6.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God establishes the Nazirite vow of special consecration and gives the priestly blessing — 'The LORD bless you and keep you.'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 6 contains two distinct but related sections:
the law of the Nazirite vow (vv.1-21) and the Aaronic Blessing (vv.22-27).
The Nazirite vow allows any Israelite — man or woman — to consecrate
themselves to God in a special way for a set period. This is remarkable:
in a system where only Levites serve the Tabernacle and only Aaron's sons
are priests, the Nazirite vow opens a path of extraordinary devotion to
any person from any tribe. It is voluntary, temporary (usually), and
intensely personal. The three restrictions — no wine/grapes, no razor, no
contact with the dead — mirror aspects of the high priest's consecration,
suggesting the Nazirite takes on a priest-like holiness. The chapter
concludes with the Aaronic Blessing (vv.24-26), one of the most beloved
passages in all Scripture — the words God commands the priests to speak
over Israel. It is God's own blessing, spoken through human lips, placing
His name upon His people.

The Nazirite Vow — Separation unto the LORD (vv.1-8): Either man or woman
may take this vow (v.2) — "to separate themselves unto the LORD." Three
requirements:
  (1) No wine, strong drink, grape juice, grapes (fresh or dried), or
      anything from the vine — "from the kernels even to the husk" (vv.3-4).
      Total abstinence from the fruit of the vine.
  (2) No razor on the head — hair grows freely as a visible sign of the
      vow (v.5). "He shall be holy."
  (3) No contact with any dead body — not even father, mother, brother, or
      sister (vv.6-7). "The consecration of his God is upon his head" (v.7).
"All the days of his separation he is holy unto the LORD" (v.8). The
Nazirite is set apart — visibly (long hair), behaviorally (no wine), and
relationally (no corpse contact). The vow is total.

Defilement and Restoration (vv.9-12): If someone dies suddenly near the
Nazirite, defiling his consecrated head (v.9): he shaves his head on the
seventh day (day of cleansing) (v.9). On the eighth day: two turtledoves
or pigeons — one for sin offering, one for burnt offering (vv.10-11).
Atonement is made (v.11). He re-consecrates and brings a lamb for a
trespass/guilt offering (v.12). Critical: "the days that were before shall
be lost" (v.12) — the previous time does not count. He starts over. The
vow cannot be partially fulfilled. Defilement resets the clock entirely.

Completion of the Vow (vv.13-21): When the days are fulfilled, the
Nazirite comes to the Tabernacle entrance (v.13). He brings: one male lamb
(burnt offering), one ewe lamb (sin offering), one ram (peace offering),
plus unleavened bread, grain offering, and drink offerings (vv.14-15). The
priest offers them (vv.16-17). The Nazirite shaves his consecrated head
and puts the hair in the fire under the peace offering (v.18) — the hair
itself becomes an offering to God. The priest places the boiled shoulder,
one cake, and one wafer in the Nazirite's hands, then waves them (vv.19-20).
"After that the Nazarite may drink wine" (v.20) — the vow is complete.
The separation ends with celebration.

The Aaronic Blessing (vv.22-27): God commands Aaron and his sons to bless
Israel with these words (v.23):
  "The LORD bless thee, and keep thee" (v.24) — provision and protection.
  "The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee" (v.25)
    — favor and grace.
  "The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace" (v.26)
    — attention and shalom.
"And they shall put my name upon the children of Israel; and I will bless
them" (v.27). The priests speak the words, but GOD does the blessing. His
name is placed upon His people — they bear His identity. Three lines, three
blessings, the name "LORD" (Yahweh) three times — many see a foreshadowing
of the Trinity.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Wilderness of Sinai: The Nazirite vow and Aaronic blessing are given while Israel is still encamped at Sinai.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Judges      | 13      | 5                  | Samson: a Nazirite from birth (lifelong vow).
v.2              | 1 Samuel    | 1       | 11                 | Hannah's vow for Samuel: "no razor shall come upon his head."
v.3              | Luke        | 1       | 15                 | John the Baptist: "shall drink neither wine nor strong drink" — Nazirite-like from birth.
v.5              | Judges      | 16      | 17                 | Samson's hair as the sign of his consecration.
v.6              | Leviticus   | 21      | 11                 | The high priest also cannot contact the dead — the Nazirite shares this restriction.
v.7              | Leviticus   | 21      | 1-4                | Ordinary priests may defile for close family; the Nazirite may not — stricter than a priest.
v.12             | Galatians   | 3       | 3                  | "Having begun by the Spirit, are you now being perfected by the flesh?" (ESV) — starting over after failure.
v.18             | Acts        | 21      | 23-26              | Paul participates in a Nazirite vow completion.
v.24             | Psalm       | 121     | 7-8                | "The LORD will keep you from all evil." (ESV)
v.25             | Psalm       | 67      | 1                  | "May God be gracious to us and bless us and make his face to shine upon us." (ESV)
v.26             | John        | 14      | 27                 | "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you." (ESV)
v.26             | Romans      | 5       | 1                  | "since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God." (ESV)
v.27             | Revelation  | 22      | 4                  | "His name will be on their foreheads." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-6/
  Guzik emphasizes that the Nazirite vow democratizes holiness — any
  Israelite can take on extraordinary consecration. He notes the three
  restrictions parallel the high priest's life, making the Nazirite a
  "lay priest." On the Aaronic Blessing, he highlights the threefold
  repetition of "the LORD" as a Trinitarian hint.

Charles Spurgeon:
  On the Aaronic Blessing: "Three times the sacred name is spoken. Three
  blessings are pronounced. The Father blesses and keeps. The Son makes
  His face to shine and is gracious. The Spirit lifts up His countenance
  and gives peace. Here is the whole Trinity engaged in blessing the
  people of God. And mark the last verse: 'I will bless them.' The priests
  speak the words, but God performs the deed. The blessing is not in the
  priest's lips but in God's hands."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "a Nazarite" / ESV: "a Nazirite" — Hebrew "nazir" — from "nazar" (to separate, consecrate). The word means "separated one" or "consecrated one." Both spellings are acceptable English transliterations.
v.3              | KJV         | "strong drink" / ESV: "strong drink" — Hebrew "shekhar" — any fermented beverage other than grape wine (beer, mead, etc.). The Nazirite abstains from ALL intoxicants, not just wine.
v.4              | KJV         | "from the kernels even to the husk" / ESV: "not even the seeds or the skins" — Hebrew "me-chartsannim v'ad zag" — from grape seeds to grape skins. Total abstinence from everything vine-related. The extremity emphasizes completeness.
v.5              | KJV         | "shall let the locks of the hair of his head grow" / ESV: "shall let the locks of hair of his head grow long" — Hebrew "gadel pera s'ar rosho" — grow the loose/free hair of his head. "Pera" means unbound, free-growing hair. The hair is the visible badge of consecration.
v.7              | KJV         | "the consecration of his God is upon his head" / ESV: "his separation to God is on his head" — Hebrew "nezer elohav al rosho" — the consecration/crown of his God is on his head. "Nezer" is the same word used for the high priest's crown/diadem (Exodus 29:6). The Nazirite's hair IS his crown.
v.12             | KJV         | "the days that were before shall be lost" / ESV: "the previous period shall be void" — Hebrew "v'ha-yamim ha-rishonim yiplu" — the former days shall fall (be lost/void). Complete reset. No partial credit.
v.18             | KJV         | "put it in the fire which is under the sacrifice of the peace offerings" / ESV: "put it on the fire that is under the sacrifice of the peace offering" — The hair — the sign of the vow — is offered to God in the fire of the peace offering. The symbol of consecration becomes a sacrifice of thanksgiving.
v.24-26          | KJV         | Both KJV and ESV render the Aaronic Blessing nearly identically. The Hebrew is three lines of increasing length: 3 words, 5 words, 7 words (in Hebrew). The structure is poetic and liturgical — designed to be spoken aloud over the congregation.
v.27             | KJV         | "they shall put my name upon the children of Israel" / ESV: "so shall they put my name upon the people of Israel" — Hebrew "v'samu et sh'mi al b'nei yisrael" — they shall place my name upon the sons of Israel. God's name is placed on His people — they are identified with Him, they belong to Him, they bear His reputation.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Nazirite (Hebrew: "nazir") | A "separated one." A person who voluntarily takes a vow of special consecration to God for a set period. Three restrictions: no vine products, no haircut, no corpse contact. Open to men and women of any tribe. Famous Nazirites: Samson (lifelong), Samuel (lifelong), Paul (temporary — Acts 18:18). | 
Separation (Hebrew: "nezer" / "nazar") | The root means to separate or consecrate. "Nezer" also means crown or diadem — the Nazirite's uncut hair is his crown of consecration, parallel to the high priest's golden crown. | 
From the kernels to the husk | Total abstinence from everything grape-related. Not merely avoiding intoxication but avoiding any association with the vine. The vine represents earthly joy and celebration — the Nazirite sets aside earthly pleasure for divine devotion. | 
Aaronic Blessing  | The three-line blessing God commands the priests to pronounce over Israel (vv.24-26). Still used in Jewish synagogues and Christian worship today. The oldest known biblical text — found on silver scrolls from the 7th century BC (Ketef Hinnom scrolls). | 
Shalom (Hebrew: "peace") | More than absence of conflict. Wholeness, completeness, well-being, harmony, prosperity. The final word of the Aaronic Blessing — the culmination of God's blessing is total shalom. | 
Put my name upon  | To place God's name on someone is to identify them as His, to claim them, to give them His protection and reputation. Like a father's name on a child. God's people bear His name — they are called by Him and belong to Him. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Voluntary consecration (v.2). The Nazirite vow is not commanded — it is
   chosen. God honors those who voluntarily set themselves apart for Him.
   What are you willing to give up — not because you must, but because you
   want to draw closer to God?

2. Visible holiness (v.5). The uncut hair is a public sign. The Nazirite's
   devotion is not hidden. Are you willing to be visibly identified with
   God, even when it draws attention or questions?

3. Total commitment (v.4). Not just "no wine" but "nothing from the vine —
   from kernels to husk." God is not interested in partial devotion. Half-
   measures insult the vow. "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with ALL thy
   heart" (Deuteronomy 6:5).

4. Failure resets the clock (v.12). If the Nazirite is defiled, previous
   days are lost. You cannot bank holiness. Past faithfulness does not
   cover present failure. But note: the vow can be restarted. Failure is
   not final — restoration is possible.

5. The Aaronic Blessing (vv.24-26). God wants to bless His people. He
   commands the priests to speak blessing — not curse, not warning, but
   BLESSING. And He Himself performs it: "I will bless them" (v.27). Rest
   in this: God's disposition toward His people is blessing. "The LORD
   bless thee, and keep thee."

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-6/

7.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The twelve tribal leaders bring identical offerings over twelve days for the Tabernacle dedication — extravagant, joyful generosity.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 7 is the longest chapter in Numbers (89 verses)
and records the dedication offerings of the twelve tribal leaders for the
altar. Chronologically, this takes place on the day the Tabernacle was
completed and anointed (Exodus 40:17 — the first day of the first month,
second year), which is actually BEFORE the census of Numbers 1 (first day
of the second month). The chapter is placed here thematically — after the
organization of the camp and the Levites, the leaders bring their offerings.
The most striking feature: all twelve offerings are IDENTICAL. Each leader
brings exactly the same items on successive days. The repetition (which
seems tedious to modern readers) makes a theological point: every tribe is
equal before God. No tribe gives more or less. No tribe is honored above
another in worship. The chapter ends with a remarkable verse (v.89): Moses
enters the Tabernacle and hears God's voice speaking from between the
cherubim on the mercy seat. The offerings are accepted — God speaks.

Wagons and Oxen for the Levites (vv.1-9): After the Tabernacle is set up
and anointed (v.1), the tribal leaders bring six covered wagons and twelve
oxen (v.3). God tells Moses to accept them for Levitical service (vv.4-5).
Distribution: Gershonites receive 2 wagons and 4 oxen (v.7) — they carry
curtains and coverings (lighter loads). Merarites receive 4 wagons and 8
oxen (v.8) — they carry frames, bars, and pillars (heavier loads). The
Kohathites receive NONE (v.9) — "because the service of the sanctuary
belonging unto them was that they should bear upon their shoulders." The
most holy things are carried by hand, not by cart. (This principle is
violated later with tragic results — 2 Samuel 6:3-7.)

The Twelve-Day Dedication (vv.10-83): God commands: one leader per day for
twelve days (v.11). The order follows the camp arrangement (Chapter 2):
  Day 1: Nahshon (Judah) — vv.12-17
  Day 2: Nethanel (Issachar) — vv.18-23
  Day 3: Eliab (Zebulun) — vv.24-29
  Day 4: Elizur (Reuben) — vv.30-35
  Day 5: Shelumiel (Simeon) — vv.36-41
  Day 6: Eliasaph (Gad) — vv.42-47
  Day 7: Elishama (Ephraim) — vv.48-53
  Day 8: Gamaliel (Manasseh) — vv.54-59
  Day 9: Abidan (Benjamin) — vv.60-65
  Day 10: Ahiezer (Dan) — vv.66-71
  Day 11: Pagiel (Asher) — vv.72-77
  Day 12: Ahira (Naphtali) — vv.78-83

Each offering is identical:
  — 1 silver plate (charger): 130 shekels, full of flour mixed with oil
  — 1 silver bowl (basin): 70 shekels, full of flour mixed with oil
  — 1 golden dish (spoon): 10 shekels, full of incense
  — 1 bull, 1 ram, 1 male lamb (burnt offering)
  — 1 male goat (sin offering)
  — 2 oxen, 5 rams, 5 male goats, 5 male lambs (peace offerings)

The Grand Totals (vv.84-88):
  Silver plates: 12 (total silver: 2,400 shekels)
  Golden dishes: 12 (total gold: 120 shekels)
  Burnt offerings: 12 bulls, 12 rams, 12 lambs
  Sin offerings: 12 male goats
  Peace offerings: 24 bulls, 60 rams, 60 male goats, 60 lambs

God Speaks from the Mercy Seat (v.89): After the dedication, Moses enters
the tent of meeting and hears God's voice "from off the mercy seat that
was upon the ark of testimony, from between the two cherubims" (v.89). The
offerings are accepted. God responds. Worship leads to communion.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Wilderness of Sinai: The tribal leaders bring offerings to the Tabernacle over 12 days. The Tabernacle is "before the tabernacle of the congregation" at Sinai.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Exodus      | 40      | 17-33              | The setting up and anointing of the Tabernacle.
v.3              | 2 Samuel    | 6       | 3-7                | David's error: putting the ark on a cart instead of carrying it on shoulders (violating the principle of v.9).
v.9              | 1 Chronicles | 15      | 2                  | , 15 — David corrects the error: "None ought to carry the ark of God but the Levites... upon their shoulders."
v.10             | 1 Kings     | 8       | 62-66              | Solomon's dedication of the Temple — a parallel dedication with massive offerings.
v.12             | Numbers     | 2       | 3                  | Nahshon of Judah leads — same order as the camp.
v.12             | Ruth        | 4       | 20                 | ; Matthew 1:4 — Nahshon in the lineage of Christ.
v.84             | 2 Chronicles | 7       | 5-9                | Solomon's dedication offerings: 22,000 oxen, 120,000 sheep.
v.89             | Exodus      | 25      | 22                 | "I will commune with thee from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubims."
v.89             | Numbers     | 12      | 8                  | God speaks to Moses "mouth to mouth."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-7/
  Guzik notes that the repetition of identical offerings emphasizes
  equality — no tribe is greater or lesser in worship. He also highlights
  v.89 as the climax: after all the offerings, God speaks. Worship is not
  one-directional; God responds to those who seek Him.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Twelve leaders, twelve days, twelve identical offerings. God records
  each one separately — He does not say 'they all brought the same thing.'
  No! He names each man and lists each gift as though it were unique. Why?
  Because to God, each offering IS unique — it comes from a different
  heart, a different hand, a different act of faith. Your offering may
  look the same as another's, but God sees the heart behind it. He records
  it individually. You are not lost in the crowd of worshippers."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "had fully set up the tabernacle" / ESV: "had finished setting up the tabernacle" — Hebrew "kalot Mosheh l'haqim" — Moses finishing to erect. Both convey completion.
v.3              | KJV         | "six covered wagons" / ESV: "six wagons" — Hebrew "shesh eglot tsav." "Tsav" may mean "covered" (KJV) or simply be a type of wagon. The ESV omits "covered"; KJV includes it. These are transport wagons for Tabernacle materials.
v.9              | KJV         | "they should bear upon their shoulders" / ESV: "had to be carried on the shoulder" — Hebrew "ba-katef yissa'u" — on the shoulder they shall carry. The holy objects have poles/bars for shoulder-carrying. No mechanical transport for the most sacred items.
v.13             | KJV         | "one silver charger" / ESV: "one silver plate" — Hebrew "qa'arat kesef achat" — one silver dish/plate. KJV's "charger" is archaic English for a large flat dish or platter. ESV modernizes to "plate." A large vessel for presenting the grain offering.
v.14             | KJV         | "one spoon of ten shekels of gold" / ESV: "one golden dish of 10 shekels" — Hebrew "kaf achat asarah zahav" — one palm/ladle of ten [shekels] gold. "Kaf" literally means "palm" — a small bowl shaped like a cupped hand. KJV's "spoon" and ESV's "dish" both approximate. Used for incense.
v.13             | KJV         | "meat offering" / ESV: "grain offering" — Hebrew "minchah." KJV uses "meat" in the archaic sense of "food" (not flesh). ESV correctly renders "grain offering" for modern readers. This is a flour-and-oil offering, not an animal sacrifice.
v.89             | KJV         | "he heard the voice of one speaking unto him" / ESV: "he heard the voice speaking to him" — Hebrew "yishma et ha-qol middaber elav" — he heard the voice speaking to him. The voice comes from the mercy seat, between the cherubim. God speaks audibly to Moses — direct, personal communication.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Dedication (Hebrew: "chanukkah") | The consecration or inauguration of the altar for service. The same Hebrew word gives us the later festival of Hanukkah (rededication of the Temple in 165 BC). To dedicate is to set apart for sacred use. | 
Charger/Plate (Hebrew: "qa'arah") | A large silver dish or platter used to present the grain offering. Weight: 130 shekels (approximately 3.25 pounds of silver). | 
Spoon/Dish (Hebrew: "kaf") | A small golden bowl (shaped like a palm) used for incense. Weight: 10 shekels of gold (approximately 4 ounces). | 
Peace Offering (Hebrew: "shelamim") | A fellowship offering shared between God (the fat), the priest (breast and shoulder), and the worshipper (the rest). It celebrates communion and thanksgiving. The largest category of animals offered here. | 
Mercy Seat (Hebrew: "kapporet") | The gold lid of the Ark of the Covenant where God's presence dwells and from which He speaks to Moses. The place where atonement blood is sprinkled (Leviticus 16). God's throne of grace. | 
Upon their shoulders | The prescribed method for transporting the most holy objects. Poles are inserted through rings on the furniture, and Kohathites carry them on their shoulders. This ensures human reverence and direct responsibility — no impersonal mechanical transport for sacred things. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Identical offerings, individual recognition (vv.12-83). God records each
   leader's offering separately — even though they are all the same. Your
   faithfulness may look ordinary, but God sees it individually. He does
   not lump His people together. Your worship is personally received.

2. No shortcuts with holy things (v.9). The Kohathites get no wagons.
   Sacred things are carried on shoulders — with effort, with care, with
   personal cost. Are you looking for an easier way to handle what God has
   entrusted to you? Some things require the weight of personal
   responsibility.

3. Equality in worship (vv.12-83). No tribe gives more or less. The largest
   tribe (Judah: 74,600) and the smallest (Manasseh: 32,200) bring the same
   offering. Worship is not measured by size or wealth but by obedience and
   heart.

4. One per day (v.11). God does not rush the dedication. Twelve days for
   twelve leaders. He gives each one a full day — unhurried, unshared
   attention. God is not in a hurry. He savors each act of worship.

5. Worship leads to hearing God (v.89). After all the offerings, Moses
   enters and hears God speak. Offering precedes revelation. Worship opens
   the ears. If you want to hear God, begin with giving — your time, your
   attention, your sacrifice.

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/numbers/

David Guzik — Numbers 7 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-7/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

8.ESV:
1. Now the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
2. “Speak to Aaron and say to him, When you set up the lamps, the seven lamps shall give light in front of the lampstand.”
3. And Aaron did so: he set up its lamps in front of the lampstand, as the LORD commanded Moses.
4. And this was the workmanship of the lampstand, hammered work of gold. From its base to its flowers, it was hammered work; according to the pattern that the LORD had shown Moses, so he made the lampstand.
5. And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
6. “Take the Levites from among the people of Israel and cleanse them.
7. Thus you shall do to them to cleanse them: sprinkle the water of purification upon them, and let them go with a razor over all their body, and wash their clothes and cleanse themselves.
8. Then let them take a bull from the herd and its grain offering of fine flour mixed with oil, and you shall take another bull from the herd for a sin offering.
9. And you shall bring the Levites before the tent of meeting and assemble the whole congregation of the people of Israel.
10. When you bring the Levites before the LORD, the people of Israel shall lay their hands on the Levites,
11. and Aaron shall offer the Levites before the LORD as a wave offering from the people of Israel, that they may do the service of the LORD.
12. Then the Levites shall lay their hands on the heads of the bulls, and you shall offer the one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering to the LORD to make atonement for the Levites.
13. And you shall set the Levites before Aaron and his sons, and shall offer them as a wave offering to the LORD.
14. “Thus you shall separate the Levites from among the people of Israel, and the Levites shall be mine.
15. And after that the Levites shall go in to serve at the tent of meeting, when you have cleansed them and offered them as a wave offering.
16. For they are wholly given to me from among the people of Israel. Instead of all who open the womb, the firstborn of all the people of Israel, I have taken them for myself.
17. For all the firstborn among the people of Israel are mine, both of man and of beast. On the day that I struck down all the firstborn in the land of Egypt I consecrated them for myself,
18. and I have taken the Levites instead of all the firstborn among the people of Israel.
19. And I have given the Levites as a gift to Aaron and his sons from among the people of Israel, to do the service for the people of Israel at the tent of meeting and to make atonement for the people of Israel, that there may be no plague among the people of Israel when the people of Israel come near the sanctuary.”
20. Thus did Moses and Aaron and all the congregation of the people of Israel to the Levites. According to all that the LORD commanded Moses concerning the Levites, the people of Israel did to them.
21. And the Levites purified themselves from sin and washed their clothes, and Aaron offered them as a wave offering before the LORD, and Aaron made atonement for them to cleanse them.
22. And after that the Levites went in to do their service in the tent of meeting before Aaron and his sons; as the LORD had commanded Moses concerning the Levites, so they did to them.
23. And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
24. “This applies to the Levites: from twenty-five years old and upward they shall come to do duty in the service of the tent of meeting.
25. And from the age of fifty years they shall withdraw from the duty of the service and serve no more.
26. They minister to their brothers in the tent of meeting by keeping guard, but they shall do no service. Thus shall you do to the Levites in assigning their duties.”
8.KJV:
1. And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
2. Speak unto Aaron, and say unto him, When thou lightest the lamps, the seven lamps shall give light over against the candlestick.
3. And Aaron did so; he lighted the lamps thereof over against the candlestick, as the LORD commanded Moses.
4. And this work of the candlestick was of beaten gold, unto the shaft thereof, unto the flowers thereof, was beaten work: according unto the pattern which the LORD had shewed Moses, so he made the candlestick.
5. And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
6. Take the Levites from among the children of Israel, and cleanse them.

8.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 8
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The lampstand is lit, and the Levites are purified and presented as a living offering to serve God in place of Israel's firstborn.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 8 contains two sections: the lighting of the
lampstand (vv.1-4) and the consecration of the Levites (vv.5-26). The
lampstand instruction connects to the Tabernacle's ongoing function — light
must continually shine in the Holy Place. The consecration of the Levites
is the practical outworking of Chapters 3-4: God claimed the Levites as
substitutes for the firstborn; now they are formally set apart for service
through an elaborate purification ceremony. The ceremony parallels priestly
consecration (Leviticus 8) but is simpler — the Levites are not priests
but servants of the priests. They are washed, shaved, sprinkled, and
presented as a "wave offering" — literally waved before the LORD as a
living sacrifice. The people of Israel lay hands on them, transferring
their obligation (the firstborn's service) to the Levites. The chapter
also clarifies the age of service: 25-50 (with Chapter 4 specifying 30-50
for the heavy transport work). After 50, they assist but do not perform
the primary service.

The Lampstand (vv.1-4): God instructs Aaron on lighting the seven lamps —
they give light "over against the candlestick" (KJV) / "in front of the
lampstand" (ESV) (v.2). Aaron obeys (v.3). The lampstand is described:
beaten gold, from base to flowers, made "according unto the pattern which
the LORD had shewed Moses" (v.4). The lampstand provides light in the Holy
Place — without it, the priests serve in darkness. Light is essential for
service. The seven lamps represent completeness of illumination.

The Cleansing of the Levites (vv.5-7): God commands: "Take the Levites...
and cleanse them" (v.6). The cleansing process: (1) sprinkle the water of
purification upon them, (2) shave all their flesh with a razor, (3) wash
their clothes (v.7). The sprinkling purifies from defilement. The shaving
symbolizes removal of the old — a fresh start, a new identity. The washing
of clothes represents outward cleanliness. Every aspect — spiritual,
physical, external — is addressed.

The Consecration Ceremony (vv.8-15): Two bulls are brought — one with a
grain offering, one for a sin offering (v.8). The Levites are brought
before the Tabernacle with the whole congregation assembled (v.9). The
people of Israel lay their hands on the Levites (v.10) — transferring
their obligation. Aaron offers the Levites "for an offering" (KJV) / "as
a wave offering" (ESV) before the LORD (v.11). The Levites then lay their
hands on the bulls' heads (v.12) — transferring their own sin. One bull
is a sin offering, one a burnt offering — atonement for the Levites (v.12).
The Levites are set before Aaron and offered as an offering to the LORD
(v.13). "Thus shalt thou separate the Levites... and the Levites shall be
mine" (v.14). After cleansing and offering, they begin their service (v.15).

The Theological Basis Restated (vv.16-19): The Levites are "wholly given"
to God (v.16) — substitutes for the firstborn (v.16). God's claim on the
firstborn dates to the Passover (v.17). The Levites replace the firstborn
(v.18). They are given as "a gift to Aaron and to his sons" (v.19) to
serve at the Tabernacle and "to make an atonement for the children of
Israel: that there be no plague among the children of Israel, when the
children of Israel come nigh unto the sanctuary" (v.19). The Levites are
a buffer — they prevent the people from approaching too closely and
incurring God's wrath.

Obedience and Completion (vv.20-22): Moses, Aaron, and the congregation
do everything as commanded (v.20). The Levites are purified, washed,
offered, and atoned for (v.21). They begin their service (v.22). Complete
obedience.

The Age of Service (vv.23-26): Levites begin service at age 25 (v.24).
They retire from active duty at age 50 (v.25). After 50, they may
"minister with their brethren... to keep the charge" but "shall do no
service" (v.26) — they assist and guard but do not perform the heavy
labor of transport. Note: Chapter 4 specifies age 30 for the transport
work; age 25 likely represents an apprenticeship period (25-30) before
full service begins.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Wilderness of Sinai: The Levites are cleansed and dedicated for service at the Tabernacle. Israel is still at Sinai preparing for departure.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Exodus      | 25      | 31-40              | The design of the golden lampstand.
v.2              | Revelation  | 1       | 12                 | 13, 20 — Christ among the seven lampstands (churches).
v.4              | Exodus      | 25      | 40                 | "Make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount."
v.7              | Numbers     | 19      | 9                  | , 17-19 — The water of purification (red heifer ashes).
v.10             | Leviticus   | 1       | 4                  | Laying hands on the sacrifice — identification and transfer.
v.11             | Romans      | 12      | 1                  | "Present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God."
v.14             | Numbers     | 3       | 12-13              | The Levites taken instead of the firstborn.
v.16             | Numbers     | 3       | 45                 | "Take the Levites instead of all the firstborn."
v.17             | Exodus      | 13      | 2                  | "Consecrate to me all the firstborn." (ESV)
v.19             | Numbers     | 1       | 53                 | "That there be no wrath upon the congregation."
v.19             | Numbers     | 16      | 46-48              | Aaron stands between the living and the dead to stop a plague.
v.24             | Numbers     | 4       | 3                  | Age 30 for transport service (full duty).
v.24             | 1 Chronicles | 23      | 24-27              | David later lowers the age to 20 (no more transport needed).
v.26             | 1 Timothy   | 4       | 14                 | "Do not neglect the gift you have, which was given you by... the laying on of hands." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-8/
  Guzik emphasizes the Levites as "living sacrifices" — offered to God as
  the people's representatives. He connects this to Romans 12:1 and the
  New Testament call for all believers to present themselves as living
  offerings. He also notes the age limits as showing God's wisdom in
  matching service to capacity.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The Levites are offered as a wave offering — living men, waved before
  God as a sacrifice. They do not die on the altar; they LIVE on the
  altar. This is what Paul means: 'Present your bodies a living sacrifice.'
  You are not called to die for God (though you may be); you are called to
  LIVE for God — every day, every hour, offered up, set apart, wholly His.
  The Levite's life was not his own. Neither is yours, believer."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "over against the candlestick" / ESV: "in front of the lampstand" — Hebrew "el mul p'nei ha-menorah" — toward the face/ front of the lampstand. The lamps are positioned to cast light forward (toward the table of showbread on the north side). KJV's "over against" means "facing toward"; ESV's "in front of" is clearer.
v.4              | KJV         | "beaten gold, unto the shaft thereof, unto the flowers thereof" / ESV: "hammered work of gold. From its base to its flowers" — Hebrew "miqshah zahav ad y'rekhah ad pirchah." The entire lampstand is hammered from a single piece of gold — base, shaft, branches, and decorative flowers. One piece, not assembled.
v.7              | KJV         | "water of purifying" / ESV: "water of purification" — Hebrew "mei chattat" — literally "water of sin [offering]." This is water mixed with the ashes of the red heifer (Numbers 19). It purifies from defilement. Both translations capture the function.
v.7              | KJV         | "let them shave all their flesh" / ESV: "let them go with a razor over all their body" — Hebrew "v'he'eviru ta'ar al kol b'saram" — pass a razor over all their flesh. Complete shaving of the body — a dramatic act of purification and new beginning. Similar to the leper's cleansing (Leviticus 14:8-9).
v.11             | KJV         | "for an offering" / ESV: "as a wave offering" — Hebrew "t'nufah" — a wave offering. The Levites are literally "waved" before the LORD — presented with the same gesture used for animal offerings. They are living sacrifices, offered to God by the community.
v.19             | KJV         | "that there be no plague" / ESV: "that there may be no plague" — Hebrew "v'lo yihyeh... negef" — and there shall not be a plague/striking. Without the Levites as intermediaries, the people's approach to the sanctuary would bring divine judgment. The Levites absorb the danger.
v.24             | KJV         | "from twenty and five years old" / ESV: "from twenty-five years old" — Hebrew "mi-ben chamesh v'esrim shanah." Age 25 for beginning service. This differs from the age 30 in 4:3. The reconciliation: age 25-30 is apprenticeship/training; age 30-50 is full active service (especially the heavy transport work).
v.26             | KJV         | "shall minister with their brethren... to keep the charge, and shall do no service" / ESV: "they minister to their brothers... by keeping guard, but they shall do no service" — Hebrew "v'sheret et echav... lishmor mishmeret, va-avodah lo ya'avod." After 50, Levites assist and guard but do not perform the primary labor. Retirement from heavy duty, not from all usefulness.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Lampstand (Hebrew: "menorah") | The seven-branched golden lampstand in the Holy Place. Hammered from a single piece of gold. Provides light for priestly service. Symbolizes God's light, His Spirit, and His illumination of truth. The seven lamps represent completeness. | 
Wave Offering (Hebrew: "t'nufah") | An offering presented by waving it before the LORD — a symbolic gesture of giving to God and receiving back. The Levites are "waved" — given to God as living offerings, then given back to serve. | 
Water of Purification | Water mixed with the ashes of the red heifer (Numbers 19). Used for cleansing from defilement, especially corpse contamination. Sprinkled on the Levites as part of their consecration. | 
Wholly given" (Hebrew: "n'tunim n'tunim") | Emphatic doubling: given, given. The Levites are completely, unreservedly given to God. They belong entirely to Him. No divided loyalty, no partial commitment. | 
Laying on of Hands | The act of placing hands on another to transfer identity, obligation, or blessing. Israel lays hands on the Levites (transferring their firstborn obligation). The Levites lay hands on the bulls (transferring their sin). The chain of substitution: people → Levites → animals. | 
Apprenticeship (ages 25 | 30) — The period between beginning service (age 25, 8:24) and full transport duty (age 30, 4:3). Five years of training and lighter duties before assuming the heavy responsibility of carrying the holy things. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Living sacrifices (v.11). The Levites are offered to God — not dead but
   alive. This is the pattern for New Testament believers: "Present your
   bodies a living sacrifice" (Romans 12:1). You are not your own. You have
   been offered to God by grace. Live accordingly.

2. Complete cleansing (v.7). Sprinkled, shaved, washed. Nothing is left
   unaddressed. God's cleansing is thorough — He does not leave pockets of
   defilement. "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive
   us our sins, and to cleanse us from ALL unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9).

3. The community's role (v.10). The people lay hands on the Levites. The
   congregation participates in setting apart those who serve. Ministry is
   not a solo act — it is commissioned by the body. Do you support and
   affirm those who serve in your community?

4. Protection through mediation (v.19). The Levites prevent plague. Without
   mediators, the people's approach to God brings death. Christ is our
   mediator — "there is one God, and one mediator between God and men" (1
   Timothy 2:5). Through Him, we approach safely.

5. Seasons of service (vv.24-26). There is a time to begin and a time to
   step back. Age 50 brings retirement from heavy labor but not from all
   usefulness. The older Levites "keep the charge" — they guard, advise,
   and support. Every season has its service.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Numbers Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/numbers/

David Guzik — Numbers 8 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-8/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

9.ESV:
1. And the LORD spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt, saying,
2. “Let the people of Israel keep the Passover at its appointed time.
3. On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, you shall keep it at its appointed time; according to all its statutes and all its rules you shall keep it.”
4. So Moses told the people of Israel that they should keep the Passover.
5. And they kept the Passover in the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, at twilight, in the wilderness of Sinai; according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so the people of Israel did.
6. And there were certain men who were unclean through touching a dead body, so that they could not keep the Passover on that day, and they came before Moses and Aaron on that day.
7. And those men said to him, “We are unclean through touching a dead body. Why are we kept from bringing the LORD’s offering at its appointed time among the people of Israel?”
8. And Moses said to them, “Wait, that I may hear what the LORD will command concerning you.”
9. The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
10. “Speak to the people of Israel, saying, If any one of you or of your descendants is unclean through touching a dead body, or is on a long journey, he shall still keep the Passover to the LORD.
11. In the second month on the fourteenth day at twilight they shall keep it. They shall eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
12. They shall leave none of it until the morning, nor break any of its bones; according to all the statute for the Passover they shall keep it.
13. But if anyone who is clean and is not on a journey fails to keep the Passover, that person shall be cut off from his people because he did not bring the LORD’s offering at its appointed time; that man shall bear his sin.
14. And if a stranger sojourns among you and would keep the Passover to the LORD, according to the statute of the Passover and according to its rule, so shall he do. You shall have one statute, both for the sojourner and for the native.”
15. On the day that the tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered the tabernacle, the tent of the testimony. And at evening it was over the tabernacle like the appearance of fire until morning.
16. So it was always: the cloud covered it by day and the appearance of fire by night.
17. And whenever the cloud lifted from over the tent, after that the people of Israel set out, and in the place where the cloud settled down, there the people of Israel camped.
18. At the command of the LORD the people of Israel set out, and at the command of the LORD they camped. As long as the cloud rested over the tabernacle, they remained in camp.
19. Even when the cloud continued over the tabernacle many days, the people of Israel kept the charge of the LORD and did not set out.
20. Sometimes the cloud was a few days over the tabernacle, and according to the command of the LORD they remained in camp; then according to the command of the LORD they set out.
21. And sometimes the cloud remained from evening until morning. And when the cloud lifted in the morning, they set out, or if it continued for a day and a night, when the cloud lifted they set out.
22. Whether it was two days, or a month, or a longer time, that the cloud continued over the tabernacle, abiding there, the people of Israel remained in camp and did not set out, but when it lifted they set out.
23. At the command of the LORD they camped, and at the command of the LORD they set out. They kept the charge of the LORD, at the command of the LORD by Moses.

1. The Lord spoke to Moses in the desert of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt:

4. So Moses instructed the Israelites to observe the Passover.
5. And they observed the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month at twilight in the desert of Sinai; in accordance with all that the Lord had commanded Moses, so the Israelites did.

9. The Lord spoke to Moses:

11. They may observe it on the fourteenth day of the second month at twilight; they are to eat it with bread made without yeast and with bitter herbs.
12. They must not leave any of it until morning, nor break any of its bones; they must observe it in accordance with every statute of the Passover.
13. “‘But the man who is ceremonially clean, and was not on a journey, and fails to keep the Passover, that person must be cut off from his people. Because he did not bring the Lord’s offering at its appointed time, that man must bear his sin.

19. When the cloud remained over the tabernacle many days, then the Israelites obeyed the instructions of the Lord and did not journey.
20. When the cloud remained over the tabernacle a number of days, they remained camped according to the Lord’s commandment, and according to the Lord’s commandment they would journey.
21. And when the cloud remained only from evening until morning, when the cloud was taken up the following morning, then they traveled on. Whether by day or by night, when the cloud was taken up they traveled.
22. Whether it was for two days, or a month, or a year that the cloud prolonged its stay over the tabernacle, the Israelites remained camped without traveling; but when it was taken up, they traveled on.
23. At the commandment of the Lord they camped, and at the commandment of the Lord they traveled on; they kept the instructions of the Lord according to the commandment of the Lord, by the authority of Moses.

9.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel celebrates the second Passover; God provides guidance through the cloud — when it moves they march, when it stays they camp.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 9 records two significant events: the second
Passover observance (vv.1-14) and the guidance of the cloud over the
Tabernacle (vv.15-23). The date is the first month of the second year —
chronologically BEFORE the census of Chapter 1 (second month). This is the
first anniversary of the Exodus, and God commands Israel to observe the
Passover again. A practical problem arises: some men are ceremonially
unclean and cannot participate. Rather than deciding on his own, Moses
waits for God's answer — and God provides a gracious solution: a second
Passover one month later for those legitimately unable to observe the first.
This shows God's desire that ALL His people participate in remembering
redemption. The second half of the chapter describes the cloud — God's
visible presence guiding Israel. When the cloud moves, they move; when it
stays, they stay. This is the pattern of the entire wilderness journey:
complete dependence on God's leading, day by day, with no advance schedule.

The Second Passover Observed (vv.1-5): God speaks in the first month of
the second year (v.1) — one month before the census. Command: keep the
Passover "at his appointed season" (v.2). The date: fourteenth day of the
first month, at evening (v.3). "According to all the rites of it, and
according to all the ceremonies thereof" (v.3) — exactly as prescribed in
Exodus 12. Moses tells the people; they obey (vv.4-5). This is only the
second Passover ever observed — the first was in Egypt. It will not be
observed again until they enter Canaan (Joshua 5:10).

The Problem of Uncleanness (vv.6-8): Certain men are defiled by contact
with a dead body and cannot keep the Passover (v.6). They come to Moses
with a remarkable question: "Wherefore are we kept back, that we may not
offer an offering of the LORD in his appointed season?" (v.7). They do not
want to be excluded — they WANT to worship. Moses does not presume to
answer: "Stand still, and I will hear what the LORD will command concerning
you" (v.8). A model of leadership: when you do not know, wait for God.

God's Gracious Provision — The Second Month Passover (vv.9-14): God
provides a solution: anyone unclean from a dead body OR on a distant
journey may keep the Passover in the second month, fourteenth day, at
evening (vv.10-11). Same requirements: unleavened bread, bitter herbs,
nothing left until morning, no bone broken (vv.11-12). But the warning:
anyone who is CLEAN and NOT traveling and still fails to keep the Passover
"shall be cut off from among his people" (v.13) — deliberate neglect of
Passover is a capital offense. The stranger/sojourner who wishes to keep
it may do so — "one ordinance, both for the stranger, and for him that was
born in the land" (v.14). God's grace provides a way; God's justice
demands participation.

The Cloud — God's Visible Guidance (vv.15-23): On the day the Tabernacle
was erected, the cloud covered it (v.15). At evening: the appearance of
fire until morning (v.15). "So it was alway" (v.16) — cloud by day, fire
by night. The pattern: when the cloud lifts, Israel journeys; where the
cloud settles, Israel camps (v.17). "At the commandment of the LORD the
children of Israel journeyed, and at the commandment of the LORD they
pitched" (v.18). Whether the cloud stayed many days (v.19), a few days
(v.20), overnight (v.21), two days, a month, or a year (v.22) — they
waited. "At the commandment of the LORD they rested... and at the
commandment of the LORD they journeyed" (v.23). No schedule. No advance
notice. Pure dependence on God's moment-by-moment leading.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Wilderness of Sinai: Israel keeps the second Passover in the wilderness (v.5). The cloud guides their movements — when it lifts, they march; when it settles, they camp.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Exodus      | 12      | 1-14               | The original Passover institution in Egypt.
v.3              | Exodus      | 12      | 6                  | "The fourteenth day... the whole assembly... shall kill it in the evening."
v.5              | Joshua      | 5       | 10                 | The next recorded Passover: upon entering Canaan.
v.8              | Psalm       | 27      | 14                 | "Wait on the LORD: be of good courage."
v.12             | Exodus      | 12      | 46                 | "Neither shall ye break a bone thereof."
v.12             | John        | 19      | 36                 | "A bone of him shall not be broken" — fulfilled in Christ.
v.13             | Hebrews     | 2       | 3                  | "How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation?"
v.14             | Ephesians   | 2       | 12-13              | Strangers brought near through Christ.
v.15             | Exodus      | 40      | 34-38              | The cloud covering the Tabernacle at its completion.
v.17             | Psalm       | 32      | 8                  | "I will instruct thee and teach thee in the way which thou shalt go."
v.18             | Proverbs    | 3       | 5-6                | "Trust in the LORD with all thine heart... and he shall direct thy paths."
v.22             | Isaiah      | 40      | 31                 | "They that wait upon the LORD shall renew their strength."
v.23             | Psalm       | 37      | 23                 | "The steps of a good man are ordered by the LORD."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-9/
  Guzik highlights the men's desire to worship as exemplary — they did not
  use their uncleanness as an excuse to skip Passover but sought a way to
  participate. He emphasizes the cloud as a picture of daily dependence on
  God: no roadmap, no itinerary, just following His presence moment by
  moment.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The cloud moved, and they moved. The cloud stayed, and they stayed.
  Whether two days or a year — they waited. Oh, for such obedience! We
  want to know the plan for the next five years. God says: follow Me
  today. We want a map; God gives us a cloud. We want certainty; God gives
  us His presence. Is that not enough? The cloud is better than a map,
  for the map cannot change, but the cloud is the living God who adapts
  His leading to every circumstance."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "in the first month of the second year" / ESV: "in the first month of the second year" — This is chronologically BEFORE Chapter 1 (which is the second month). The chapter is placed here thematically, not chronologically.
v.3              | KJV         | "at even" / ESV: "at twilight" — Hebrew "bein ha-arbayim" — literally "between the evenings." This phrase is debated: either between noon and sunset (when the sun declines) or between sunset and dark. The Passover lamb is killed in this window. KJV's "even" and ESV's "twilight" both approximate.
v.7              | KJV         | "wherefore are we kept back" / ESV: "why are we kept from bringing" — Hebrew "lamah nigara" — why are we diminished/held back? The men feel deprived, not relieved. They want to participate in worship, not be excused from it.
v.12             | KJV         | "nor break any bone of it" / ESV: "nor break any of its bones" — Hebrew "v'etzem lo yishb'ru vo" — a bone they shall not break in it. This detail points to Christ: "A bone of him shall not be broken" (John 19:36; Psalm 34:20). The Passover lamb's unbroken bones foreshadow the crucified Christ.
v.13             | KJV         | "shall be cut off from among his people" / ESV: "shall be cut off from his people" — Hebrew "v'nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi me-ammeha" — that soul shall be cut off from its people. "Cut off" (karet) may mean execution, excommunication, or divine judgment (premature death). The penalty is severe — Passover is not optional.
v.15             | KJV         | "the tent of the testimony" / ESV: "the tent of the testimony" — Hebrew "ohel ha-edut" — the tent of the testimony (the tablets of the Law). The cloud covers the structure that houses God's covenant words.
v.22             | KJV         | "or a year" / ESV: "or a longer time" — Hebrew "o yamim" — or days (an indefinite period). KJV interprets "yamim" as "a year" (which it can mean — cf. Leviticus 25:29); ESV renders it more generally as "a longer time." The point: whether short or long, they waited.
v.23             | KJV         | "at the commandment of the LORD" / ESV: "at the command of the LORD" — Hebrew "al pi Yahweh" — literally "at the mouth of the LORD." God's command is His spoken word — His "mouth." The cloud is the visible expression of God's verbal command.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Passover (Hebrew: "Pesach") | The annual feast commemorating Israel's deliverance from Egypt (Exodus 12). The lamb is killed on the 14th of the first month (Nisan/Abib). It remembers the night God "passed over" the houses marked with blood. Christ is "our passover... sacrificed for us" (1 Corinthians 5:7). | 
Cut off" (Hebrew: "karet") | A severe penalty for deliberate violation of covenant obligations. May refer to execution, divine judgment (premature death), or permanent exclusion from the community. Applied to those who willfully neglect Passover. | 
The Cloud (Hebrew: "he | anan") — The visible manifestation of God's presence (the Shekinah glory). Cloud by day, fire by night. It covers the Tabernacle, guides the march, and signals when to move or stay. It is God Himself leading His people. | 
Between the evenings" (Hebrew: "bein ha | arbayim") — The time for killing the Passover lamb. Debated: either afternoon (noon to sunset) or twilight (sunset to dark). The lamb dies as the day transitions — between light and darkness. | 
Appointed Season (Hebrew: "mo'ed") | A fixed time, an appointed meeting. God's festivals are "appointments" — set times when He meets with His people. The Passover is not optional or flexible (except for the gracious provision of the second month). | 
Sojourner/Stranger (Hebrew: "ger") | A non-Israelite living among Israel. The sojourner may participate in Passover — the same law applies. God's redemption is not ethnically exclusive. Foreshadows Gentile inclusion in Christ. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Hunger for worship (v.7). These men did not want to be excused — they
   wanted to participate. Do you approach God's ordinances with that same
   hunger? Or do you look for reasons to skip, to be excused, to stay home?

2. God provides a way (vv.10-11). For those with legitimate obstacles, God
   makes provision. He does not exclude the willing — He finds a way. If
   you have been kept from worship by circumstances beyond your control,
   God has not forgotten you. He makes a way.

3. Neglect is not neutral (v.13). The clean man who simply "forbears" to
   keep Passover is cut off. Neglect of God's grace is not passive — it is
   active rebellion. "How shall we escape, if we neglect so great
   salvation?" (Hebrews 2:3).

4. Following the cloud (vv.17-22). No schedule. No advance notice. Just
   follow. This is the life of faith: "Trust in the LORD with all thine
   heart; and lean not unto thine own understanding" (Proverbs 3:5). Can
   you wait when the cloud stays? Can you move when the cloud lifts — even
   if you just unpacked?

5. "At the commandment of the LORD" (v.23). This phrase repeats throughout
   the passage. Every movement, every rest — at God's command. The
   surrendered life does not make its own schedule. It follows.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Numbers Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/numbers/

David Guzik — Numbers 9 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-9/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

10.ESV:
1. The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
2. “Make two silver trumpets. Of hammered work you shall make them, and you shall use them for summoning the congregation and for breaking camp.
3. And when both are blown, all the congregation shall gather themselves to you at the entrance of the tent of meeting.
4. But if they blow only one, then the chiefs, the heads of the tribes of Israel, shall gather themselves to you.
5. When you blow an alarm, the camps that are on the east side shall set out.
6. And when you blow an alarm the second time, the camps that are on the south side shall set out. An alarm is to be blown whenever they are to set out.
7. But when the assembly is to be gathered together, you shall blow a long blast, but you shall not sound an alarm.
8. And the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall blow the trumpets. The trumpets shall be to you for a perpetual statute throughout your generations.
9. And when you go to war in your land against the adversary who oppresses you, then you shall sound an alarm with the trumpets, that you may be remembered before the LORD your God, and you shall be saved from your enemies.
10. On the day of your gladness also, and at your appointed feasts and at the beginnings of your months, you shall blow the trumpets over your burnt offerings and over the sacrifices of your peace offerings. They shall be a reminder of you before your God: I am the LORD your God.”
11. In the second year, in the second month, on the twentieth day of the month, the cloud lifted from over the tabernacle of the testimony,
12. and the people of Israel set out by stages from the wilderness of Sinai. And the cloud settled down in the wilderness of Paran.
13. They set out for the first time at the command of the LORD by Moses.
14. The standard of the camp of the people of Judah set out first by their companies, and over their company was Nahshon the son of Amminadab.
15. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Issachar was Nethanel the son of Zuar.
16. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Zebulun was Eliab the son of Helon.
17. And when the tabernacle was taken down, the sons of Gershon and the sons of Merari, who carried the tabernacle, set out.
18. And the standard of the camp of Reuben set out by their companies, and over their company was Elizur the son of Shedeur.
19. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Simeon was Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai.
20. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Gad was Eliasaph the son of Deuel.
21. Then the Kohathites set out, carrying the holy things, and the tabernacle was set up before their arrival.
22. And the standard of the camp of the people of Ephraim set out by their companies, and over their company was Elishama the son of Ammihud.
23. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Manasseh was Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur.
24. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Benjamin was Abidan the son of Gideoni.
25. Then the standard of the camp of the people of Dan, acting as the rear guard of all the camps, set out by their companies, and over their company was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai.
26. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Asher was Pagiel the son of Ochran.
27. And over the company of the tribe of the people of Naphtali was Ahira the son of Enan.
28. This was the order of march of the people of Israel by their companies, when they set out.
29. And Moses said to Hobab the son of Reuel the Midianite, Moses’ father-in-law, “We are setting out for the place of which the LORD said, ‘I will give it to you.’ Come with us, and we will do good to you, for the LORD has promised good to Israel.”
30. But he said to him, “I will not go. I will depart to my own land and to my kindred.”
31. And he said, “Please do not leave us, for you know where we should camp in the wilderness, and you will serve as eyes for us.
32. And if you do go with us, whatever good the LORD will do to us, the same will we do to you.”
33. So they set out from the mount of the LORD three days’ journey. And the ark of the covenant of the LORD went before them three days’ journey, to seek out a resting place for them.
34. And the cloud of the LORD was over them by day, whenever they set out from the camp.
35. And whenever the ark set out, Moses said, “Arise, O LORD, and let your enemies be scattered, and let those who hate you flee before you.”
36. And when it rested, he said, “Return, O LORD, to the ten thousand thousands of Israel.”
