24.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 24
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Balaam delivers his final oracles, blessing Israel a third and fourth time and prophesying a star and scepter rising from Jacob.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 24 contains Balaam's third and fourth oracles
— the climax of the Balaam narrative. In the third oracle (vv.3-9), Balaam
abandons his divination practices entirely and the Spirit of God comes upon
him directly. He pronounces Israel's beauty, abundance, and military
supremacy, culminating in a direct echo of the Abrahamic covenant: "Blessed
is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee" (v.9). Balak
is furious and dismisses Balaam. But before departing, Balaam delivers a
fourth oracle (vv.15-24) — the most far-reaching and messianic of all:
"There shall come a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre shall rise out of
Israel" (v.17). This is one of the earliest and clearest messianic
prophecies in the Pentateuch, pointing to a future King who will arise from
Israel to exercise universal dominion. The Star and Sceptre imagery has
been understood as referring to David in the near term and to Christ in the
ultimate fulfillment. The chapter also contains brief oracles against
Amalek, the Kenites, Asshur, and Eber (vv.20-24) — a panoramic sweep of
future history. Balaam then departs — but not before later counseling Balak
to seduce Israel through Moabite women (Numbers 31:16), revealing that his
heart remained corrupt despite the true words God forced through his mouth.

Third Oracle: Israel's Beauty and Power (vv.1-9): A significant shift:
Balaam "went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments" (v.1) — he
abandons divination and faces the wilderness directly. "The spirit of God
came upon him" (v.2) — genuine divine inspiration, not occult manipulation.
He introduces himself: "the man whose eyes are open... which heard the
words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty" (vv.3-4). The oracle:
"How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob!" (v.5) — Israel's encampment is
beautiful, orderly, blessed. Imagery of abundance: valleys, gardens,
rivers, aloes, cedars (v.6). "His king shall be higher than Agag, and his
kingdom shall be exalted" (v.7) — a future king greater than the Amalekite
king (fulfilled in Saul's defeat of Agag, 1 Samuel 15). God's strength:
"the strength of an unicorn [wild ox]" (v.8). Israel as a lion: "who shall
stir him up?" (v.9). The climax echoes Genesis 12:3: "Blessed is he that
blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee" (v.9). Balak strikes
his hands in fury (v.10) and dismisses Balaam (v.11).

Fourth Oracle: The Star and Sceptre (vv.14-19): Before departing, Balaam
offers a final oracle — "what this people shall do to thy people in the
latter days" (v.14). His introduction is expanded: he now "knew the
knowledge of the most High" (v.16). The great prophecy: "I shall see him,
but not now: I shall behold him, but not nigh" (v.17) — a future figure,
distant in time. "There shall come a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre
shall rise out of Israel" (v.17). The Star = a royal figure, a light, a
guide. The Sceptre = a king, a ruler, sovereign authority. This figure
will "smite the corners of Moab, and destroy all the children of Sheth"
(v.17). "Edom shall be a possession" (v.18). "Out of Jacob shall come he
that shall have dominion" (v.19). Near fulfillment: David conquers Moab
and Edom (2 Samuel 8:2, 14). Ultimate fulfillment: Christ, the Star of
Bethlehem (Matthew 2:2), the King of kings who exercises universal dominion.

Oracles Against the Nations (vv.20-24): Amalek: "the first of the nations;
but his latter end shall be that he perish for ever" (v.20) — Amalek was
first to attack Israel (Exodus 17:8); they will be utterly destroyed
(fulfilled under Saul and David, completed under Hezekiah; 1 Chronicles
4:43). The Kenites: "Strong is thy dwellingplace" but "shall be wasted,
until Asshur shall carry thee away captive" (vv.21-22). A final oracle:
"ships shall come from the coast of Chittim [Kittim], and shall afflict
Asshur, and shall afflict Eber" (v.24) — a distant prophecy, possibly
referring to Greek/Roman power from the west overwhelming Mesopotamian
empires. "Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!" (v.23).

Balaam Departs (v.25): "Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his
place: and Balak also went his way" (v.25). Balaam leaves — but Numbers
31:16 reveals he later counseled Balak to use Moabite women to seduce
Israel into idolatry (the sin of Baal-peor, chapter 25). He could not
curse them with words, so he corrupted them with women. He dies by
Israel's sword (Numbers 31:8).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- The top of Peor (continuing from ch. 23): Balaam looks down at Israel's camp on the plains of Moab arranged by tribes.
- The "Star out of Jacob" prophecy (v.17) points to a future ruler from Israel who will crush Moab and Edom.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Numbers     | 11      | 25                 | The Spirit comes upon the seventy elders — the same Spirit now on Balaam.
v.7              | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 8-9                | Saul defeats Agag king of the Amalekites — partial fulfillment of v.7.
v.9              | Genesis     | 12      | 3                  | "I will bless those who bless you, and him who dishonors you I will curse" (ESV) — the Abrahamic covenant.
v.9              | Genesis     | 49      | 9                  | "Judah is a lion's cub... who dares rouse him?" (ESV) up?" — the same lion imagery.
v.17             | Genesis     | 49      | 10                 | "The sceptre shall not depart from Judah... until Shiloh come" — another messianic sceptre prophecy.
v.17             | 2 Samuel    | 8       | 2                  | , 14 — David conquers Moab and Edom — near fulfillment.
v.17             | Matthew     | 2       | 2                  | "Where is he that is born King of the Jews? for we have seen his star in the east" — the Magi follow the Star.
v.17             | Revelation  | 22      | 16                 | "I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star."
v.17             | Revelation  | 2       | 27-28              | "He shall rule them with a rod of iron... I will give him the morning star."
v.19             | Psalm       | 72      | 8                  | "He shall have dominion also from sea to sea."
v.19             | Daniel      | 7       | 14                 | "There was given him dominion, and glory, and a kingdom."
v.20             | Exodus      | 17      | 8-16               | Amalek's first attack on Israel; God's vow to blot out their memory.
v.20             | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 2-3                | God commands Saul to destroy Amalek completely.
v.24             | Daniel      | 11      | 30                 | "Ships of Chittim shall come against him" — possibly the same prophecy in Daniel's vision.
v.25             | Numbers     | 31      | 8                  | , 16 — Balaam's death and his counsel to corrupt Israel through sexual sin.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-24/
  Guzik emphasizes verse 17 as one of the most important messianic
  prophecies in the Pentateuch. He notes that the Star and Sceptre imagery
  combines light (revelation, guidance) with authority (kingship, rule).
  The Magi in Matthew 2 may have known this prophecy — Balaam was from
  their region (Mesopotamia). He also notes the tragic irony: Balaam
  speaks the most beautiful prophecy of Christ but later leads Israel into
  the worst sin of the wilderness period.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'A Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre out of Israel.' Here is Christ in
  the wilderness, prophesied by a man who will not follow Him. The Star
  shines in the darkness — and the darkness comprehends it not. Balaam
  sees the Star from afar: 'I shall see him, but not now; I shall behold
  him, but not nigh.' He sees the future King but will not bow to Him. He
  prophesies the Sceptre but will not submit to its rule. How many see
  Christ clearly and perish! How many speak of Him eloquently and are lost!
  It is not seeing the Star that saves — it is following it. It is not
  knowing the Sceptre that redeems — it is bowing to it."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "to seek for enchantments" / ESV: "to look for omens" — Hebrew "liqrat n'chashim." "Nechashim" = enchantments, omens, divination signs. Previously Balaam used divination techniques; now he abandons them and receives direct revelation from God's Spirit. A significant shift in method, though not in heart.
v.3              | KJV         | "the man whose eyes are open" / ESV: "the man whose eye is opened" — Hebrew "sh'tum ha-ayin." "Shetum" is debated: it may mean "opened" (seeing clearly) or "closed" (in trance, with inner eyes opened). Most translations favor "opened" — Balaam claims spiritual sight. The irony: the man whose donkey saw more than he did now claims open eyes.
v.4              | KJV         | "falling into a trance, but having his eyes open" / ESV: "falling down with his eyes uncovered" — Hebrew "nofel u-g'luy einayim." Balaam falls prostrate (overwhelmed by the vision) but his eyes remain open — he sees the vision while conscious. This describes ecstatic prophetic experience.
v.7              | KJV         | "his king shall be higher than Agag" / ESV: "his king shall be higher than Agag" — "Agag" was a title (like Pharaoh) for Amalekite kings. Israel's future king will surpass even the greatest of the Amalekite rulers. Fulfilled when Saul defeats Agag (1 Samuel 15).
v.17             | KJV         | "a Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre shall rise out of Israel" / ESV: "a star shall come out of Jacob, and a scepter shall rise out of Israel" — Hebrew "darakh kokhav mi-Ya'aqov v'qam shevet mi-Yisra'el." "Kokhav" = star. "Shevet" = sceptre, rod, tribe. The Star and Sceptre are parallel — both refer to the same royal figure. "Darakh" = to tread, march forth — the Star actively comes.
v.17             | KJV         | "shall smite the corners of Moab" / ESV: "shall crush the forehead of Moab" — Hebrew "u-machatz pa'atei Mo'av." "Pa'atei" can mean "corners/borders" or "temples/forehead" (sides of the head). KJV reads geographically (borders of Moab); ESV reads physically (crushing the skull). Both convey total defeat.
v.17             | KJV         | "destroy all the children of Sheth" / ESV: "break down all the sons of Sheth" — Hebrew "v'qarqar kol b'nei Sheth." "Sheth" may refer to Seth (all humanity as descendants of Seth) or to a Moabite people/place. If "all the sons of Seth" = all humanity, this is a prophecy of universal dominion — the Messiah rules all.
v.24             | KJV         | "ships... from the coast of Chittim" / ESV: "ships... from Kittim" — "Kittim" originally referred to Cyprus, then broadly to the western Mediterranean coastlands (Greece, Rome). This prophecy looks far into the future: western naval powers will afflict Assyria and the descendants of Eber (Hebrews/Semites). Possibly fulfilled by Alexander the Great or Rome.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Star out of Jacob | A messianic title. Stars represent royalty, glory, and divine guidance. A king will arise from Jacob's line who shines with divine authority. Near fulfillment: David. Ultimate fulfillment: Christ — "the bright and morning star" (Revelation 22:16). The Magi followed a literal star to find the newborn King (Matthew 2:2). | 
Sceptre out of Israel | A symbol of royal authority and sovereign rule. The sceptre represents the right to govern. Combined with Genesis 49:10 ("the sceptre shall not depart from Judah"), this points to an eternal King from Israel who exercises universal dominion. Fulfilled in Christ's kingship. | 
The Latter Days" (v.14) | Hebrew "b'acharit ha-yamim" — in the end of days, in the future time. A prophetic formula pointing to the distant future — beyond the immediate historical context. The Star/Sceptre prophecy reaches to the coming of Messiah and beyond. | 
Agag              | A royal title for Amalekite kings (like "Pharaoh" for Egyptian kings). Israel's king will be "higher than Agag" — greater than the greatest pagan ruler. Fulfilled when Saul defeats Agag (1 Samuel 15), and ultimately in Christ who is "King of kings" (Revelation 19:16). | 
Kittim / Chittim  | Originally Cyprus; later used broadly for western Mediterranean peoples (Greeks, Romans). Ships from Kittim represent western military power. In Daniel 11:30, "ships of Kittim" refers to Roman intervention. Balaam's prophecy sweeps across centuries of future history. | 
Children of Sheth / Sons of Sheth | Debated: either a Moabite clan/place, or all humanity (as descendants of Seth, Adam's son). If the latter, the Sceptre's dominion is universal — He rules all the sons of men. This supports the messianic interpretation pointing to Christ's universal kingship. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "A Star out of Jacob, and a Sceptre out of Israel" (v.17). The Messiah
   is both Star (light, beauty, guidance) and Sceptre (authority, rule,
   judgment). Christ is not merely a gentle guide — He is a sovereign King.
   Have you submitted to His sceptre, or only admired His star?

2. "I shall see him, but not now" (v.17). Balaam sees the Messiah from afar
   — across centuries — but will never know Him personally. You can see
   Christ clearly in prophecy and theology and still miss Him relationally.
   Head knowledge without heart surrender is Balaam's tragedy.

3. "Blessed is he that blesseth thee" (v.9). How you treat God's people
   determines how God treats you. This principle runs from Genesis 12:3
   through all of history. Bless what God blesses; do not curse what God
   has declared blessed.

4. Balaam abandons enchantments (v.1). When he stops manipulating and
   simply faces God, the Spirit comes. Sometimes our spiritual techniques
   and formulas are obstacles to genuine encounter. Stop performing and
   simply turn your face toward God.

5. "How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob!" (v.5). God sees beauty in His
   people that they cannot see in themselves. Israel is a wilderness camp
   of complaining, rebellious people — and God calls them "goodly." He sees
   you through covenant eyes, not through the lens of your failures.

6. Balaam's tragedy (v.25). He speaks the most beautiful prophecy of Christ
   — then goes home and counsels Israel's destruction through sexual sin
   (31:16). A man can have the clearest theology and the most corrupt
   heart. Truth in the mouth without truth in the life is the most
   dangerous spiritual condition. Balaam dies by the sword of the very
   people he blessed (31:8).

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Numbers Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/numbers/

David Guzik — Numbers 24 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-24/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

25.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 25
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel sins with Moabite women and worships Baal at Peor; a plague kills 24,000 until Phinehas' zeal for God stops the judgment.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 25 records one of Israel's darkest hours — the
sin at Baal-Peor. Having failed to curse Israel through Balaam's oracles
(chapters 22-24), the enemy succeeds through seduction. Revelation 2:14
reveals that Balaam counseled Balak to use Moabite and Midianite women to
entice Israel into sexual immorality and idolatry. The strategy works:
Israel "yokes" itself to Baal of Peor, provoking God's fierce anger. A
plague kills 24,000 before Phinehas, grandson of Aaron, acts decisively —
driving a spear through an Israelite man and a Midianite woman in the very
act of their sin. God rewards Phinehas with a "covenant of peace" and a
"covenant of perpetual priesthood." This chapter demonstrates that what
Balaam's curses could not accomplish, Israel's own sin nearly achieves:
their destruction. The enemy's most effective weapon against God's people
is not external attack but internal corruption.

Israel's Sin at Shittim (vv.1-3): Israel dwells at Shittim (Abel-shittim,
"meadow of acacias") in the plains of Moab, directly across from Jericho.
The people "began to commit whoredom" (KJV) / "began to whore" (ESV) with
Moabite women — both literal sexual immorality and spiritual adultery
through idol worship. The women invite Israel to their sacrificial feasts
(v.2) — eating, bowing down, and "yoking" themselves to Baal of Peor (v.3).
"Baal-Peor" means "lord of Peor" — a local deity worshiped at Mount Peor
with sexually immoral rites. The progression is deliberate: social contact
→ sexual immorality → religious participation → covenant with a false god.

God's Judgment and Moses' Response (vv.4-5): God commands Moses to "take
all the heads of the people and hang them up before the LORD against the
sun" (KJV) — public execution of the ringleaders as a deterrent. Moses
instructs the judges to execute those who joined themselves to Baal-Peor
(v.5). The judgment is swift and public — sin in the covenant community
demands corporate accountability.

Phinehas' Zeal (vv.6-9): In brazen defiance, an Israelite man (later
identified as Zimri, a Simeonite prince) brings a Midianite woman (Cozbi,
daughter of a Midianite chief) into the camp "in the sight of Moses and
in the sight of the whole congregation" (v.6) — while the people are
weeping at the tabernacle entrance over the plague already underway.
Phinehas, son of Eleazar, son of Aaron, rises from the congregation, takes
a spear, follows them into the tent, and pierces both through. The plague
stops — but not before 24,000 have died (v.9).

God's Covenant with Phinehas (vv.10-13): God declares that Phinehas "turned
back my wrath" because "he was zealous for my sake among them" (KJV) /
"he was jealous with my jealousy" (ESV). God gives him: (1) "my covenant
of peace" (v.12) — ironic and beautiful: the man of the spear receives
peace; and (2) "the covenant of an everlasting priesthood" (v.13) — his
line will serve as priests perpetually. Phinehas "made atonement" for
Israel — his act of righteous judgment turned away God's consuming wrath.

The Guilty Named (vv.14-15): The Israelite man: Zimri son of Salu, "a
prince of a chief house among the Simeonites" (v.14). The Midianite woman:
Cozbi daughter of Zur, "head over a people" in Midian (v.15). Both are
nobility — this was a deliberate, high-level act of defiance, not a
private sin of common people.

Command Against Midian (vv.16-18): God commands Moses to "vex" (KJV) /
"harass" (ESV) the Midianites and strike them — "for they vex you with
their wiles" (v.18). The Midianites used deception ("wiles") as a weapon
— seduction as warfare. This command is fulfilled in chapter 31.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Shittim / Plains of Moab (v.1): "Abel-shittim" — Israel is camped here when they sin with the Moabite women and worship Baal-peor.
- Baal-peor: A local shrine/deity near Israel's camp on the plains of Moab, east of the Jordan.

Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference

v.4              | Deuteronomy | 21      | 22-23              | Public hanging as a sign of God's curse.

v.9              | 1 Corinthians | 10      | 8                  | Paul says 23,000 fell "in one day" — the difference of 1,000 may account for those executed by judges (v.5) versus those who died by plague.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-25/
  Guzik emphasizes that Balaam's strategy succeeded where his curses failed.
  Satan's most effective weapon against God's people is not persecution but
  seduction. He notes that Phinehas' act was not vigilante justice but
  righteous execution of God's already-declared sentence (v.4-5).

Charles Spurgeon:
  "What the devil cannot do by force, he will attempt by fraud. Balaam
  could not curse Israel, so he corrupted them. The sword that could not
  pierce them from without was thrust into their own hands to destroy
  themselves. Beware, Christian — your greatest danger is not the roaring
  lion but the subtle serpent. The world that cannot defeat you by
  persecution will embrace you to death. Phinehas teaches us that there
  is a holy anger, a righteous zeal, that God not only permits but rewards.
  Indifference to sin is not grace — it is complicity."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "began to commit whoredom" / ESV: "began to whore" — Hebrew "vayyachel ha-am liznot." "Zanah" means to commit fornication or to be unfaithful. Both translations capture the meaning; ESV is more direct. The term carries both literal (sexual) and figurative (spiritual adultery/idolatry) meaning.
v.3              | KJV         | "Israel joined himself unto Baal-peor" / ESV: "Israel yoked himself to Baal of Peor" — Hebrew "vayyitzamed Yisrael l'Baal Peor." "Tzamad" means to yoke, bind, or attach. ESV's "yoked" is more vivid — like an animal bound to a plow. The imagery suggests servitude to a foreign god.
v.4              | KJV         | "Take all the heads of the people, and hang them up before the LORD against the sun" / ESV: "Take all the chiefs of the people and hang them in the sun before the LORD" — Hebrew "roshei ha-am." "Roshei" means heads/chiefs/leaders. The ambiguity: are the "heads" the guilty leaders to be executed, or the tribal chiefs who must carry out execution? Most commentators read it as the guilty ringleaders being publicly executed.
v.7              | KJV         | "took a javelin in his hand" / ESV: "took a spear in his hand" — Hebrew "romach." A romach is a large thrusting spear or lance, not a throwing javelin. ESV's "spear" is more accurate to the Hebrew. KJV's "javelin" suggests a throwing weapon, but Phinehas thrust it through both people at close range.
v.8              | KJV         | "into the tent" / ESV: "into the chamber" — Hebrew "ha-qubbah." This word occurs only here in the OT. It likely refers to an inner room, alcove, or tent-chamber — possibly a domed structure. ESV's "chamber" captures the sense of a private inner space.
v.11             | KJV         | "while he was zealous for my sake" / ESV: "in that he was jealous with my jealousy" — Hebrew "b'qan'o et qin'ati." The root "qana" means both zeal and jealousy. ESV preserves the wordplay: Phinehas was jealous WITH God's jealousy — he shared God's own passionate possessiveness over His people. KJV's "zealous for my sake" is accurate but loses the intensity of the doubled root.
v.17             | KJV         | "Vex the Midianites" / ESV: "Harass the Midianites" — Hebrew "tzaror." Means to bind, restrict, show hostility toward, treat as an enemy. "Harass" (ESV) suggests ongoing pressure; "Vex" (KJV) suggests irritation. The Hebrew implies treating them as adversaries — active, sustained hostility.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Shittim / Abel    | shittim — "Acacias" or "meadow of acacias." Israel's final camp east of the Jordan, in the plains of Moab opposite Jericho. The staging ground for the conquest of Canaan (Joshua 2:1; 3:1). | 
Baal              | Peor — "Lord of Peor." A local Moabite/Midianite deity worshiped at Mount Peor. Worship involved sexual immorality as a religious rite. The name becomes shorthand for Israel's apostasy (Deuteronomy 4:3; Hosea 9:10; Psalm 106:28). | 
Phinehas          | Son of Eleazar, grandson of Aaron. His name may mean "mouth of brass" or "dark-skinned." His zealous act earns him a perpetual priesthood. His line continues through Zadok and serves in Solomon's temple and beyond. | 
Covenant of Peace | God's grant to Phinehas: a covenant guaranteeing peace between God and Phinehas' line. Paradoxically given for an act of violence — because righteous judgment that removes sin restores peace between God and His people. | 
Zimri             | Son of Salu, a prince (chief) of the tribe of Simeon. His brazen sin — bringing a Midianite woman into the camp publicly during the plague — represents the height of defiance. Simeon's tribe decreases dramatically between the two censuses (59,300 to 22,200) — possibly due to heavy involvement in the Baal-Peor sin. | 
Cozbi             | Daughter of Zur, a Midianite tribal chief. Her name may mean "deceiver" or "my lie." She was likely sent deliberately as part of the Midianite strategy to corrupt Israel. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. What curses cannot accomplish, seduction can (vv.1-3). Israel withstood
   Balaam's curses (chapters 22-24) but fell to Moabite women. The enemy's
   most dangerous weapon is not opposition but invitation. What
   "invitations" from the world are more dangerous to your faith than
   outright persecution?

2. The progression of sin (vv.1-3): social contact → sexual immorality →
   idolatrous worship → covenant with a false god. Sin rarely begins at the
   end — it begins with seemingly innocent associations. Where are you in
   this progression?

3. Brazen sin in the sight of the congregation (v.6). Zimri's defiance was
   public and deliberate — while others wept in repentance, he paraded his
   sin. When sin becomes shameless, judgment is near. Do you grieve over
   sin, or have you grown comfortable with it?

4. One man's zeal stops the plague (vv.7-8). Phinehas did not wait for a
   committee or consensus. He saw the sin, understood the stakes, and
   acted. Sometimes faithfulness requires immediate, decisive action — not
   endless deliberation.

5. "Jealous with my jealousy" (v.11). God is not indifferent to unfaith-
   fulness. His jealousy is holy love that will not share its beloved with
   rivals. Do you share God's jealousy for His glory, or are you tolerant
   of what offends Him?

6. The covenant of peace through atonement (vv.12-13). Phinehas' act
   foreshadows Christ: righteous judgment against sin that produces peace
   between God and man. True peace is not the absence of conflict but the
   resolution of guilt. Christ's cross is the ultimate "spear" that
   destroys sin and secures eternal peace.

26. The sons of Zebulun after their families: of Sered, the family of the Seredites; of Elon, the family of the Elonites; of Jahleel, the family of the Jahleelites.
27. These are the families of the Zebulunites according to those that were numbered of them, threescore thousand and five hundred.
28. The sons of Joseph after their families: Manasseh and Ephraim.
29. The sons of Manasseh: of Machir, the family of the Machirites; and Machir begat Gilead; of Gilead, the family of the Gileadites.
30. These are the sons of Gilead: [of] Iezer, the family of the Iezerites; of Helek, the family of the Helekites;
31. and [of] Asriel, the family of the Asrielites; and [of] Shechem, the family of the Shechemites;
32. and [of] Shemida, the family of the Shemidaites; and [of] Hepher, the family of the Hepherites.
33. And Zelophehad the son of Hepher had no sons, but daughters: and the names of the daughters of Zelophehad were Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
34. These are the families of Manasseh; and they that were numbered of them were fifty and two thousand and seven hundred.

43. All the families of the Shuhamites, according to those that were numbered of them, were threescore and four thousand and four hundred.

48. The sons of Naphtali after their families: of Jahzeel, the family of the Jahzeelites; of Guni, the family of the Gunites;

50. These are the families of Naphtali according to their families; and they that were numbered of them were forty and five thousand and four hundred.

55. Notwithstanding, the land shall be divided by lot: according to the names of the tribes of their fathers they shall inherit.
56. According to the lot shall their inheritance be divided between the more and the fewer.

58. These are the families of Levi: the family of the Libnites, the family of the Hebronites, the family of the Mahlites, the family of the Mushites, the family of the Korahites. And Kohath begat Amram.
59. And the name of Amram`s wife was Jochebed, the daughter of Levi, who was born to Levi in Egypt: and she bare unto Amram Aaron and Moses, and Miriam their sister.

26.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 26
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A second census numbers the new generation at 601,730 — nearly the same total, confirming God's faithfulness despite forty years of death.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 26 records the second census of Israel — taken
"after the plague" of Baal-Peor (25:9). Nearly 40 years have passed since
the first census at Sinai (chapter 1). The entire generation that refused
to enter Canaan at Kadesh-barnea has now died in the wilderness, exactly
as God decreed (14:29-35). This new census counts the generation that will
actually conquer and inherit the Promised Land. The total: 601,730 fighting
men — remarkably close to the first census total of 603,550 (a decrease of
only 1,820). God has preserved His people despite 38 years of judgment.
The census also establishes the basis for land distribution: larger tribes
receive larger portions. The chapter ends with the solemn confirmation:
"among these there was not a man of them whom Moses and Aaron the priest
numbered... in the wilderness of Sinai" (v.64) — except Caleb and Joshua.
God's word of judgment was fulfilled to the letter.

The Census Commanded (vv.1-4): After the plague, God commands Moses and
Eleazar (Aaron's son and successor as high priest) to number all males
twenty years and older who can go to war. The location: the plains of Moab
by the Jordan at Jericho — they are on the doorstep of the Promised Land.

The Tribes Numbered (vv.5-51):
  Reuben: 43,730 (was 46,500 — decreased by 2,770)
  Simeon: 22,200 (was 59,300 — decreased by 37,100! — the largest decline)
  Gad: 40,500 (was 45,650 — decreased by 5,150)
  Judah: 76,500 (was 74,600 — increased by 1,900) — largest tribe
  Issachar: 64,300 (was 54,400 — increased by 9,900)
  Zebulun: 60,500 (was 57,400 — increased by 3,100)
  Manasseh: 52,700 (was 32,200 — increased by 20,500! — largest growth)
  Ephraim: 32,500 (was 40,500 — decreased by 8,000)
  Benjamin: 45,600 (was 35,400 — increased by 10,200)
  Dan: 64,400 (was 62,700 — increased by 1,700)
  Asher: 53,400 (was 41,500 — increased by 11,900)
  Naphtali: 45,400 (was 53,400 — decreased by 8,000)
  TOTAL: 601,730 (was 603,550 — net decrease of 1,820)

Notable: Simeon's catastrophic decline (63% loss) likely reflects heavy
involvement in the Baal-Peor sin — Zimri was a Simeonite prince (25:14).

Historical Notes Within the Census (vv.8-11): The census pauses to recall
Dathan and Abiram's rebellion with Korah (chapter 16) — "they became a
warning" (ESV) / "a sign" (KJV). But "the sons of Korah did not die"
(v.11) — their descendants later write Psalms (42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88).

Zelophehad's Daughters Introduced (v.33): Zelophehad of Manasseh "had no
sons, but daughters" — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah. Their
case will be addressed in chapter 27.

Land Distribution Principles (vv.52-56): Land is divided by two principles:
(1) proportional to tribe size — larger tribes get more land; and (2) by
lot — God sovereignly determines location. Size determines quantity; lot
determines placement.

The Levites (vv.57-62): Levites numbered separately: 23,000 males from one
month old (was 22,000 — increased by 1,000). They receive no land
inheritance — God is their inheritance.

The Solemn Conclusion (vv.63-65): "Not one of them was left, except Caleb
the son of Jephunneh and Joshua the son of Nun" (v.65). The entire first
generation has perished — God's word of judgment fulfilled completely.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Plains of Moab: The second census is taken here, east of Jordan, preparing for the conquest of Canaan. The old generation has perished in the wilderness.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Numbers     | 1       | 1-3                | The first census at Sinai, 38 years earlier.
v.9              | Numbers     | 16      | 1-35               | Korah's rebellion: Dathan, Abiram, and 250 leaders consumed.
v.10             | Numbers     | 16      | 31-35              | "The earth opened her mouth and swallowed them."
v.11             |             |         |                    | Psalms 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88 — Psalms written by the sons of Korah — grace from the line of a rebel.
v.14             | Numbers     | 25      | 14                 | Zimri the Simeonite prince — Simeon's massive decline likely connected to Baal-Peor involvement.
v.33             | Numbers     | 27      | 1-11               | Zelophehad's daughters petition for inheritance.
v.55             |             |         |                    | Joshua 14-19 — The actual land distribution by lot under Joshua.
v.59             | Exodus      | 2       | 1-10               | Jochebed, mother of Moses, Aaron, and Miriam.
v.61             | Leviticus   | 10      | 1-2                | Nadab and Abihu's "strange fire."
v.64             | Numbers     | 14      | 29-35              | God's decree: all numbered at Sinai (20+) would die in the wilderness.
v.65             | Numbers     | 14      | 24                 | , 30 — Caleb and Joshua exempted for their faith.
v.65             | Hebrews     | 3       | 16-19              | "They could not enter in because of unbelief."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-26/
  Guzik notes the remarkable stability of Israel's total population despite
  38 years of judgment — God preserved the nation even while disciplining
  it. He highlights Simeon's dramatic decline as evidence of corporate
  consequences for tribal sin.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Here is a new generation standing where their fathers fell. The bones
  of six hundred thousand unbelievers bleach in the desert sand, and their
  children stand on the border of Canaan. Let the young take warning: your
  fathers' unbelief cost them everything. But let them also take courage:
  God's promise outlives the generation that rejected it. He will have a
  people in His land. If one generation refuses, He raises another. The
  promise is not cancelled — only the participants change."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "as the LORD commanded Moses and the children of Israel, which went forth out of the land of Egypt" / ESV: "as the LORD commanded Moses. The people of Israel who came out of the land of Egypt were:" — The Hebrew is ambiguous in its sentence division. ESV treats "The people of Israel who came out of Egypt" as the beginning of the census list. KJV connects it to the command. ESV's reading flows more naturally into the tribal listing that follows.
v.9              | KJV         | "which were famous in the congregation" / ESV: "chosen from the congregation" — Hebrew "qeru'ei ha-edah." "Qeru'ei" means "called ones" or "summoned ones" — those called to leadership. KJV's "famous" captures their notoriety; ESV's "chosen" captures their appointed status. Both are legitimate readings.
v.10             | KJV         | "they became a sign" / ESV: "they became a warning" — Hebrew "vayyihyu l'nes." "Nes" can mean sign, banner, or warning signal. ESV interprets the purpose (warning); KJV translates more literally (sign). The context supports "warning" — their fate serves as a cautionary example.
v.11             | KJV         | "Notwithstanding the children of Korah died not" / ESV: "But the sons of Korah did not die" — Identical meaning. This parenthetical note preserves an important theological point: judgment on the father does not automatically condemn the children (cf. Ezekiel 18:20).
v.54             | KJV         | "To many thou shalt give the more inheritance, and to few thou shalt give the less inheritance" / ESV: "To a large tribe you shall give a large inheritance, and to a small tribe you shall give a small inheritance" — ESV clarifies that "many" and "few" refer to tribal population size, not individuals. The principle: proportional allocation based on need.
v.61             | KJV         | "when they offered strange fire before the LORD" / ESV: "when they offered unauthorized fire before the LORD" — Hebrew "esh zarah." "Zarah" means foreign, strange, unauthorized. ESV's "unauthorized" is more precise — the issue was not that the fire was exotic but that it was not commanded. KJV's "strange" is more literal but less clear to modern readers.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Plains of Moab    | The flat area east of the Jordan River, opposite Jericho. Israel's final encampment before crossing into Canaan. The setting for much of Deuteronomy and the end of Numbers. | 
Census (Hebrew: "paqad") | To number, muster, appoint. More than a head count — it is a military muster of fighting men and an administrative ordering of the nation for inheritance. | 
Lot (Hebrew: "goral") | A means of determining God's will, likely using marked stones or sticks. Not random chance but divine direction: "The lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Proverbs 16:33). | 
Sons of Korah     | Descendants of the rebel Korah who were spared in the judgment of Numbers 16. They became temple musicians and worship leaders. Their psalms include some of the most beloved in Scripture (Psalms 42, 84, 87). | 
Zelophehad        | A man of Manasseh who died in the wilderness (not in Korah's rebellion) leaving only daughters. His case becomes the basis for inheritance law regarding women (ch. 27, 36). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. A new generation on the border (vv.1-4). The old generation died in
   unbelief; the new generation stands ready to inherit. God's purposes are
   not defeated by one generation's failure — He raises up another. Are you
   part of the generation that refuses, or the one that enters?

2. "They became a warning" (v.10). Korah's rebellion is preserved in the
   census record as a cautionary tale. God memorializes both faith and
   failure. What "warnings" from the past are you heeding — or ignoring?

3. "The sons of Korah did not die" (v.11). Grace breaks the cycle of
   generational sin. You are not condemned to repeat your parents'
   failures. The sons of rebels became psalmists. God can redeem any
   lineage.

4. Simeon's catastrophic decline (v.14). Sin has corporate consequences.
   One prince's brazen sin (Zimri, 25:14) may reflect a tribal culture of
   compromise. The choices of leaders affect entire communities.

5. The land divided by lot AND by proportion (vv.53-56). God's sovereignty
   (the lot) works alongside practical wisdom (proportional allocation).
   Divine guidance does not eliminate human responsibility — it works
   through it.

6. "Not one was left" (v.65). God's word of judgment was fulfilled
   completely — and so will His word of promise. The same faithfulness that
   executed judgment now guarantees inheritance. If God keeps His hard
   words, He certainly keeps His good ones.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Numbers 26 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-26/

27.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 27
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The daughters of Zelophehad receive an inheritance ruling, and God commissions Joshua as Moses' successor to lead Israel into the land.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapter 27 contains two momentous events: (1) the
daughters of Zelophehad successfully petition for inheritance rights when
no male heir exists (vv.1-11), and (2) Joshua is publicly commissioned as
Moses' successor (vv.12-23). Both events look forward to the conquest and
settlement of Canaan. The daughters' petition demonstrates that God's law
is not rigid legalism but living justice — capable of addressing new
situations while preserving core principles. Moses' replacement demonstrates
that no human leader is indispensable — God's work continues beyond any
individual. Moses, who struck the rock in anger (20:7-12), is told he will
see the Promised Land but not enter it. His response is not bitterness but
selfless concern: "Let the LORD... set a man over the congregation" (v.16).
His final act of leadership is ensuring the people have a shepherd after him.

The Daughters of Zelophehad (vv.1-4): Five sisters — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah,
Milcah, and Tirzah — come before Moses, Eleazar, the princes, and the
whole congregation at the tabernacle entrance. Their father Zelophehad
(of Manasseh) died in the wilderness — not in Korah's rebellion but "in
his own sin" (the general sin of the wilderness generation). He had no
sons. They ask: "Why should the name of our father be taken away from his
clan because he had no son? Give to us a possession among our father's
brothers" (v.4). Their request is bold, public, legally reasoned, and
faithful — they believe the land promise enough to claim their share.

God's Ruling (vv.5-11): Moses brings their case before the LORD. God's
verdict: "The daughters of Zelophehad speak right" (KJV) / "are right"
(ESV). God establishes a new statute of inheritance: (1) if no son, the
daughter inherits; (2) if no daughter, the brothers inherit; (3) if no
brothers, the father's brothers inherit; (4) if no paternal uncles, the
nearest kinsman inherits. The principle: inheritance must not be lost from
a family — the name and portion must be preserved.

Moses Told of His Death (vv.12-14): God commands Moses to ascend Mount
Abarim (the mountain range including Nebo/Pisgah) and view the Promised
Land. After seeing it, he will be "gathered to his people" — a euphemism
for death that implies reunion with the departed. The reason: "you rebelled
against my word... failing to uphold me as holy at the waters" (ESV) —
the sin at Meribah-Kadesh (20:7-12) where Moses struck the rock in anger
instead of speaking to it.

Moses' Selfless Response (vv.15-17): Moses does not argue, plead, or
complain. His immediate concern is for the people: "Let the LORD, the God
of the spirits of all flesh, appoint a man over the congregation" (v.16).
He asks for a shepherd — "that the congregation of the LORD may not be as
sheep that have no shepherd" (v.17). Moses' greatest moment may be this
one: accepting God's discipline while ensuring God's people are cared for.

Joshua Commissioned (vv.18-23): God chooses Joshua son of Nun — "a man in
whom is the Spirit" (v.18). Moses lays hands on him publicly before Eleazar
and the congregation, investing him with "some of" his authority (v.20) —
not all. Joshua will not have Moses' direct access to God; he must inquire
through the Urim via Eleazar the priest (v.21). The leadership structure
shifts: Moses spoke face-to-face with God; Joshua will depend on the
priestly oracle. Moses was prophet, priest-like mediator, and leader;
Joshua will be military commander under priestly guidance.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Plains of Moab: Israel is camped in the plains of Moab (opposite Jericho). Joshua is commissioned here as Moses' successor.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Numbers     | 26      | 33                 | Zelophehad's daughters first mentioned in the census.
v.1              | Numbers     | 36      | 1-12               | The follow-up ruling: they must marry within their tribe to preserve tribal land boundaries.
v.3              | Numbers     | 16      | 1-35               | Korah's rebellion — their father was NOT part of it (they clarify this to strengthen their case).
v.7              | Joshua      | 17      | 3-6                | The daughters receive their inheritance under Joshua, as promised.
v.12             | Deuteronomy | 3       | 23-27              | Moses pleads to enter the land; God refuses but lets him see it.
v.12             | Deuteronomy | 34      | 1-5                | Moses views the land from Pisgah and dies.
v.14             | Numbers     | 20      | 7-12               | The sin at Meribah: Moses strikes the rock.
v.16             | Hebrews     | 12      | 9                  | God as "Father of spirits."
v.17             | Matthew     | 9       | 36                 | Jesus sees crowds "as sheep having no shepherd."
v.17             | John        | 10      | 11                 | "I am the good shepherd."
v.17             | 1 Peter     | 5       | 2-4                | Elders as shepherds under the Chief Shepherd.
v.18             | Deuteronomy | 34      | 9                  | "Joshua... was full of the spirit of wisdom; for Moses had laid his hands upon him."
v.20             | Deuteronomy | 31      | 7-8                | Moses publicly charges Joshua.
v.21             | Exodus      | 28      | 30                 | The Urim and Thummim in the high priest's breastplate — means of divine guidance.
v.21             | 1 Samuel    | 28      | 6                  | Saul inquires of the LORD by Urim, but receives no answer.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-27/
  Guzik highlights the daughters' faith: they believed in the promise of
  the land so strongly that they demanded their share before it was even
  conquered. He also notes that Moses' gracious response to his own
  exclusion reveals a man transformed from the angry rock-striker of
  chapter 20.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Moses asks for a shepherd — not a king, not a general, not a politician
  — a shepherd. This is what God's people always need: one who feeds, leads,
  protects, and gathers. Moses knew that Israel's greatest danger was not
  Canaanite armies but leaderless wandering. A flock without a shepherd
  scatters, starves, and is devoured. Christ is the answer to Moses' prayer
  — the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, who will never
  be 'gathered to His people' and leave them orphaned."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "died in his own sin" / ESV: "died for his own sin" — Hebrew "b'chet'o met." Both translations are valid. The point: Zelophehad died as part of the general wilderness judgment (the generation that would not enter), not as a specific rebel against God's authority. His daughters clarify this to show their father was not uniquely wicked.
v.7              | KJV         | "The daughters of Zelophehad speak right" / ESV: "The daughters of Zelophehad are right" — Hebrew "ken b'not Tzelophchad doverot." "Ken" means "right, correct, true." God validates their claim with a single word of affirmation before establishing the law.
v.14             | KJV         | "ye rebelled against my commandment... to sanctify me at the water before their eyes" / ESV: "you rebelled against my word... failing to uphold me as holy at the waters before their eyes" — Hebrew "m'ritem pi... l'haqdisheni." ESV's "failing to uphold me as holy" captures the essence: Moses' sin was misrepresenting God's character before the people. He made God look angry when God wanted to look gracious.
v.16             | KJV         | "the God of the spirits of all flesh" / ESV: "the God of the spirits of all flesh" — Hebrew "Elohei ha-ruchot l'khol basar." Identical in both translations. A rare divine title emphasizing God's sovereignty over every living soul — He knows who is fit to lead because He knows every spirit.
v.18             | KJV         | "a man in whom is the spirit" / ESV: "a man in whom is the Spirit" — Hebrew "ish asher ruach bo." ESV capitalizes "Spirit" (referring to the Holy Spirit); KJV uses lowercase "spirit" (which could mean human spirit/character or divine Spirit). The context and Deuteronomy 34:9 ("full of the spirit of wisdom") support the ESV's interpretation — this is the Spirit of God.
v.20             | KJV         | "thou shalt put some of thine honour upon him" / ESV: "You shall invest him with some of your authority" — Hebrew "v'natattah me-hodekha alav." "Hod" means splendor, majesty, authority, honor. ESV's "authority" is functional; KJV's "honour" is more literal. The key word is "some" (me- = "from/part of") — Joshua receives a portion, not the fullness, of Moses' role.
v.21             | KJV         | "the judgment of Urim" / ESV: "the judgment of the Urim" — The Urim (and Thummim) were objects in the high priest's breastplate used to discern God's will. Joshua, unlike Moses, will not speak face-to-face with God but must seek guidance through the priestly oracle — a significant limitation.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Zelophehad        | A man of the tribe of Manasseh (through Hepher, Gilead, Machir) who died in the wilderness leaving five daughters and no sons. His case establishes inheritance law for women. | 
Daughters of Zelophehad | Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah. Their bold petition before the entire congregation results in a new statute of inheritance. They receive land in Joshua 17:3-6. | 
Mount Abarim      | A mountain range east of the Jordan, including Mount Nebo and Pisgah. Moses ascends here to view the Promised Land before his death (Deuteronomy 34:1). | 
Meribah           | Kadesh — "Strife at Kadesh." The location where Moses struck the rock instead of speaking to it (Numbers 20:7-12), the sin that bars him from entering Canaan. | 
Joshua (Hebrew: Yehoshua) | "The LORD is salvation." Son of Nun, of the tribe of Ephraim. Moses' assistant since youth (Exodus 24:13), one of the two faithful spies (Numbers 14:6-9), and now commissioned as Moses' successor. A type of Christ — the one who actually brings God's people into their inheritance. | 
Urim and Thummim  | "Lights and Perfections." Sacred objects in the high priest's breastplate used to determine God's will. Their exact nature is unknown — possibly marked stones. They represent God's guidance for leaders who lack prophetic direct revelation. | 
Laying on of Hands | The act of transferring authority, blessing, or commissioning. Moses lays hands on Joshua to publicly transfer leadership. The practice continues in the NT (Acts 6:6; 1 Timothy 4:14; 2 Timothy 1:6). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Bold faith claims the promise (vv.1-4). The daughters of Zelophehad did
   not passively accept exclusion — they brought their case before God.
   They believed the land promise so firmly that they demanded their share
   before it was conquered. Do you approach God's promises with that kind
   of confident expectation?

2. God says "they are right" (v.7). God is not threatened by honest
   questions or bold petitions. He welcomes those who come with legitimate
   needs and faith-filled requests. The law is not a cage — it is a
   framework for justice that can address new situations.

3. Moses accepts discipline without bitterness (vv.12-14). He will not
   enter the land — and he does not argue. His response is not self-pity
   but selfless concern for the people. Maturity is accepting consequences
   gracefully while continuing to serve faithfully.

4. "As sheep which have no shepherd" (v.17). Moses' deepest concern is not
   his own legacy but the people's welfare. True leadership thinks about
   succession — who will care for the flock after me? Are you investing in
   the next generation of leaders?

5. "A man in whom is the Spirit" (v.18). The essential qualification for
   spiritual leadership is not education, charisma, or experience — it is
   the Spirit of God. Without the Spirit, all other qualifications are
   insufficient.

6. "Some of your authority" (v.20). Joshua receives a portion, not the
   fullness, of Moses' role. No successor perfectly replicates the
   predecessor. Each leader has their own measure and their own
   limitations. Only Christ holds all authority without limitation (Matthew
   28:18).

------------------------------------

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/numbers/

David Guzik — Numbers 27 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-27/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

28.notes:
Numbers - Chapter 28
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God reiterates the daily, weekly, and monthly offerings — a rhythm of continual worship that keeps Israel's relationship with Him at the center.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Numbers is the fourth book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Bemidbar"
— "In the wilderness." The book records Israel's 38 years of wilderness
wandering between Sinai and the Promised Land — a journey that should have
taken 11 days (Deuteronomy 1:2) but took 40 years due to unbelief.

Historical Context: Chapters 28-29 form a unit prescribing the complete
sacrificial calendar for Israel — the regular offerings that structure
worship life in the Promised Land. These instructions are given to the new
generation (after the second census of chapter 26) as they prepare to enter
Canaan. While similar offerings were prescribed at Sinai (Exodus 29:38-42;
Leviticus 23), this restatement emphasizes their ongoing obligation in the
land. The offerings move from most frequent to least: daily (morning and
evening), weekly (Sabbath), monthly (new moon), and annual festivals
(Passover/Unleavened Bread, Feast of Weeks/Firstfruits). Chapter 28 covers
daily through Feast of Weeks; chapter 29 covers the seventh-month festivals.
The key phrase repeated throughout: "a pleasing aroma to the LORD" (ESV) /
"a sweet savour unto the LORD" (KJV) — these offerings are God's "food"
(v.2), not because He needs sustenance, but because they represent
communion between God and His people. The regularity of sacrifice teaches
that access to God requires constant atonement — a truth fulfilled in
Christ's once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 7:27; 10:1-14).

The Daily Offering — Tamid (vv.1-8): God calls these "my offering, my
bread" (v.2) — language of personal relationship. The continual burnt
offering: two unblemished yearling lambs daily — one in the morning, one
at twilight (evening). Each accompanied by a grain offering (1/10 ephah
of fine flour mixed with 1/4 hin of beaten oil) and a drink offering
(1/4 hin of wine/strong drink poured out in the Holy Place). This offering
was "ordained at Mount Sinai" (v.6) — it has been in effect since Exodus
29:38-42. The tamid ("continual") offering frames every day with sacrifice
— Israel's first and last act each day is worship.

The Sabbath Offering (vv.9-10): On the Sabbath: two additional yearling
lambs (beyond the daily two), with double grain and drink offerings. The
Sabbath offering is "beside the continual burnt offering" — it adds to,
not replaces, the daily sacrifice. The Sabbath doubles the daily worship.

The Monthly Offering — New Moon (vv.11-15): At the beginning of each month:
two young bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs (all as burnt offerings),
with proportional grain offerings (3/10 ephah per bull, 2/10 per ram,
1/10 per lamb) and drink offerings (1/2 hin per bull, 1/3 per ram, 1/4
per lamb). Plus one male goat as a sin offering. All "beside the continual
burnt offering" — the monthly adds to the daily.

Passover and Unleavened Bread (vv.16-25): The 14th of the first month:
Passover. The 15th through 21st: Feast of Unleavened Bread. Days 1 and 7
are holy convocations (no ordinary work). Daily for seven days: same
offerings as the new moon (2 bulls, 1 ram, 7 lambs + 1 goat for sin
offering) — all beside the daily tamid. The Passover lamb itself is not
mentioned here (it was a household offering, not a public altar sacrifice).

Feast of Weeks / Firstfruits (vv.26-31): "The day of the firstfruits" —
also called Feast of Weeks (Shavuot) or Pentecost (50 days after
Firstfruits). A holy convocation with no ordinary work. Offerings: 2 bulls,
1 ram, 7 yearling lambs as burnt offerings + 1 male goat for sin offering.
The "new grain offering" (v.26) celebrates the wheat harvest — the first
fruits belong to God.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Plains of Moab: Offering laws given in preparation for worship in the Promised Land. "My offering, and my bread" (v.2) — God claims the firstfruits of the land they will inhabit.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Leviticus   | 3       | 11                 | "the food of the offering by fire to the LORD." (ESV) God's "food" — anthropomorphic language for communion.
v.3              | Exodus      | 29      | 38-42              | The original institution of the daily offering.
v.3              | John        | 1       | 29                 | "Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world."
v.3              | 1 Peter     | 1       | 19                 | "A lamb without blemish and without spot."
v.7              | Philippians | 2       | 17                 | Paul's life as a "drink offering" poured out.
v.9              | Isaiah      | 58      | 13                 | The Sabbath as "a delight."
v.10             | Hebrews     | 4       | 9                  | "There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God."
v.16             | Exodus      | 12      | 1-14               | The original Passover institution.
v.16             | 1 Corinthians | 5       | 7                  | "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us."
v.18             | Leviticus   | 23      | 5-8                | Passover and Unleavened Bread in Leviticus.
v.26             | Acts        | 2       | 1                  | Pentecost: the Holy Spirit poured out on the Feast of Weeks — the "firstfruits" of the Spirit (Romans 8:23).
v.26             | Leviticus   | 23      | 15-21              | Feast of Weeks in Leviticus.
v.31             | Hebrews     | 10      | 1-4                | "It is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins" — these offerings point forward.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-28/
  Guzik emphasizes the relentless regularity of the sacrificial system —
  every morning and evening, every week, every month, every festival. The
  sheer volume of blood and sacrifice taught Israel that sin is serious
  and access to God is costly. All of it points to Christ's sufficient,
  once-for-all sacrifice.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Morning and evening, a lamb. Day after day, year after year, the knife
  fell and the blood flowed. Was God satisfied? Never — for the next
  morning required another lamb. The repetition was the confession of
  insufficiency. But when the Lamb of God hung upon the cross, He cried
  'It is finished!' — and no morning lamb was ever needed again. The
  continual offering has found its end in the one offering that continues
  forever."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "My offering, and my bread for my sacrifices made by fire, for a sweet savour unto me" / ESV: "My offering, my food for my food offerings, my pleasing aroma" — Hebrew "qorbani lachmi l'ishai re'ach nichochi." "Lechem" (bread/food) is used metaphorically — God does not eat (Psalm 50:12-13). KJV's "bread" is more literal; ESV's "food" is clearer for modern readers. Both "sweet savour" (KJV) and "pleasing aroma" (ESV) translate "re'ach nichoach" — the scent of satisfaction/acceptance.
v.4              | KJV         | "at even" / ESV: "at twilight" — Hebrew "bein ha-arbayim" (literally "between the two evenings"). This phrase is debated: either between noon and sunset, or between sunset and dark. ESV's "twilight" captures the general sense; KJV's "even" (evening) is traditional.
v.5              | KJV         | "a meat offering" / ESV: "a grain offering" — Hebrew "minchah." In KJV English, "meat" meant "food" generally (not flesh). The minchah is a grain/flour offering. ESV's "grain offering" prevents confusion for modern readers. This is a significant KJV archaism.
v.7              | KJV         | "the strong wine" / ESV: "a drink offering of strong drink" — Hebrew "shekhar." Shekhar is fermented drink (beer, strong wine, or other intoxicant). It is poured out to God, not consumed by the worshiper. The drink offering symbolizes life poured out completely.
v.14             | KJV         | "half an hin of wine unto a bullock" / ESV: "half a hin of wine for a bull" — A hin is approximately 6 quarts/liters. The drink offerings are proportional to the animal's size: 1/2 hin for a bull, 1/3 for a ram, 1/4 for a lamb.
v.18             | KJV         | "ye shall do no manner of servile work" / ESV: "You shall not do any ordinary work" — Hebrew "kol m'lekhet avodah lo ta'asu." "Servile work" (KJV) means laborious/occupational work. ESV's "ordinary work" is clearer — regular daily labor ceases, but festival preparation (cooking, etc.) is permitted.
v.26             | KJV         | "in the day of the firstfruits, when ye bring a new meat offering unto the LORD, after your weeks be out" / ESV: "On the day of the firstfruits, when you offer a grain offering of new grain to the LORD at your Feast of Weeks" — ESV explicitly names the festival ("Feast of Weeks"); KJV describes it ("after your weeks be out"). Both refer to Shavuot/Pentecost — 50 days after the wave sheaf of Firstfruits.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Tamid (Continual Offering) | The daily burnt offering of two lambs, one morning and one evening. The word "tamid" means "continual, perpetual, regular." It formed the backbone of Israel's worship — the fire on the altar was never to go out (Leviticus 6:13). | 
Burnt Offering (Hebrew: "olah") | "That which ascends." An offering entirely consumed by fire — nothing kept by the worshiper or priest. Represents total consecration and acceptance before God. | 
Grain Offering (Hebrew: "minchah") | Fine flour mixed with oil, sometimes baked. Represents the fruit of human labor offered to God. Always accompanies the burnt offering — dedication of both life (animal) and livelihood (grain). | 
Drink Offering (Hebrew: "nesek") | Wine or strong drink poured out at the base of the altar. Represents life poured out completely — not consumed but given entirely to God. | 
Ephah             | A dry measure of approximately 22 liters (about 3/5 bushel). A tenth of an ephah (an "omer") is roughly 2 quarts of flour. | 
Hin               | A liquid measure of approximately 6 liters (about 1.5 gallons). The drink offerings use fractions: 1/2, 1/3, and 1/4 hin. | 
Sweet Savour / Pleasing Aroma (Hebrew: "re'ach nichoach") | Literally "scent of rest/satisfaction." Not that God smells smoke, but that the offering brings Him satisfaction — it is accepted and pleasing. The same phrase describes Noah's sacrifice (Genesis 8:21). | 
Holy Convocation (Hebrew: "miqra qodesh") | A sacred assembly; a called gathering for worship. Work ceases and the community assembles before God. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "My offering... in their due season" (v.2). God prescribes both the
   content and the timing of worship. Worship is not merely spontaneous
   emotion — it is disciplined, regular, and ordered. Do you have a "due
   season" for meeting with God, or only when you feel like it?

2. Morning and evening, every day (vv.3-4). The day begins and ends with
   sacrifice. Before any work is done, a lamb dies. Before sleep comes,
   blood is shed. The Christian life is similarly framed: begin and end
   each day at the cross.

3. The Sabbath doubles the offering (vv.9-10). Rest is not less worship but
   more. The day of ceasing from labor is the day of increased sacrifice.
   True rest is found not in inactivity but in intensified communion with
   God.

4. The relentless repetition (vv.3, 9, 11, 16, 26). Daily, weekly, monthly,
   annually — the sacrifices never stop. This repetition confesses
   insufficiency: no single offering is enough. Only Christ's once-for-all
   sacrifice ends the cycle (Hebrews 10:11-14).

5. "Without blemish" — repeated throughout. Every animal must be perfect.
   God accepts no defective worship. This points to Christ, "a lamb without
   blemish and without spot" (1 Peter 1:19). Are you offering God your
   best, or your leftovers?

6. The drink offering poured out (v.7). Wine poured at the altar — not
   consumed but given entirely. Paul describes his life as a drink offering
   (Philippians 2:17; 2 Timothy 4:6). Total self-giving, holding nothing
   back. Is your life being poured out for God's purposes?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Numbers Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/numbers/

David Guzik — Numbers 28 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/numbers-28/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/
